1→# Task Metrics and Baselines
     2→
     3→> **Crate:** `roko-learn` · **Modules:** `task_metric.rs`, `baseline.rs`, `efficiency.rs`
     4→> **Persistence:** `.roko/learn/task-metrics.jsonl`, `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl`
     5→> **Wiring:** `LearningRuntime::record_completed_run()` → metrics pipeline
     6→> **Cross-references:** [00-episode-logger](00-episode-logger.md), [07-regression-detection](07-regression-detection.md), [15-collective-calibration-31x](15-collective-calibration-31x.md)
     7→
     8→
     9→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Purpose
    14→
    15→The task metrics and baselines subsystem provides the quantitative foundation for all performance evaluation in Roko. Every gate execution produces one immutable `TaskMetric` record. These records accumulate in an append-only JSONL file, and the baseline computation groups them by `(role, complexity_band)` to produce per-slice statistical profiles. The regression detector then compares fresh batches against these baselines to identify performance degradation.
    16→
    17→The efficiency module extends per-turn instrumentation with prompt-level attribution, tool utilization tracking, and A-D letter grading for prompt assembly quality.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## TaskMetric Schema
    22→
    23→The canonical `TaskMetric` struct lives in `roko-core::metric` and is re-exported by `roko-learn::task_metric`:
    24→
    25→```rust
    26→pub struct TaskMetric {
    27→    /// Task identifier.
    28→    pub task_id: String,
    29→    /// Plan identifier.
    30→    pub plan_id: String,
    31→    /// Agent role (e.g. "Implementer").
    32→    pub role: String,
    33→    /// Complexity band ("fast", "standard", "complex").
    34→    pub complexity_band: String,
    35→    /// Model slug used.
    36→    pub model: String,
    37→    /// Backend provider.
    38→    pub backend: String,
    39→    /// Gate name.
    40→    pub gate: String,
    41→    /// Whether the gate passed.
    42→    pub gate_passed: bool,
    43→    /// Zero-based iteration index.
    44→    pub iteration: u32,
    45→    /// Cost in USD.
    46→    pub cost_usd: f64,
    47→    /// Duration in milliseconds.
    48→    pub duration_ms: u64,
    49→    /// Input tokens.
    50→    pub input_tokens: u64,
    51→    /// Output tokens.
    52→    pub output_tokens: u64,
    53→    /// Configuration hash for A/B comparison.
    54→    pub config_hash: ConfigHash,
    55→    /// Timestamp.
    56→    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    57→}
    58→```
    59→
    60→### MetricFilter
    61→
    62→The `MetricFilter` provides declarative filtering over the metric stream:
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→pub struct MetricFilter {
    66→    pub roles: HashSet<String>,
    67→    pub complexity_bands: HashSet<String>,
    68→    pub plan_ids: HashSet<String>,
    69→    pub gates: HashSet<String>,
    70→    pub models: HashSet<String>,
    71→    pub backends: HashSet<String>,
    72→    pub gate_passed: Option<bool>,
    73→    pub iteration_range: Option<(u32, u32)>,
    74→    pub min_cost_usd: Option<f64>,
    75→    pub max_cost_usd: Option<f64>,
    76→    pub config_hashes: HashSet<String>,
    77→}
    78→```
    79→
    80→All predicates are AND-combined: a record must match every non-empty filter field.
    81→
    82→### MetricsWriter and MetricsReader
    83→
    84→- `MetricsWriter` — thread-safe, append-only JSONL writer that batches records in memory and flushes to an `AsyncWrite` sink. Uses `parking_lot::Mutex` for serialization.
    85→- `MetricsReader` — parse JSONL lines from bytes, tolerant of corrupted lines (same resilience pattern as the episode logger).
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## Baseline Computation
    90→
    91→The `baseline` module computes per-slice statistical profiles from accumulated `TaskMetric` records.
    92→
    93→### SliceBaseline
    94→
    95→```rust
    96→pub struct SliceBaseline {
    97→    /// Role for this slice.
    98→    pub role: String,
    99→    /// Complexity band for this slice.
   100→    pub complexity_band: String,
   101→    /// Gate pass rate (0.0 – 1.0).
   102→    pub pass_rate: f64,
   103→    /// Average cost in USD per task.
   104→    pub avg_cost: f64,
   105→    /// Average duration in milliseconds.
   106→    pub avg_duration_ms: f64,
   107→    /// Average number of iterations to pass.
   108→    pub avg_iterations: f64,
   109→    /// Average input tokens per turn.
   110→    pub avg_input_tokens: f64,
   111→    /// Average output tokens per turn.
   112→    pub avg_output_tokens: f64,
   113→    /// Average cache hit rate.
   114→    pub avg_cache_hit_rate: f64,
   115→    /// Number of records in this slice.
   116→    pub n_records: usize,
   117→}
   118→```
   119→
   120→### Baseline
   121→
   122→```rust
   123→pub struct Baseline {
   124→    /// Per-(role, complexity) statistical profiles.
   125→    pub slices: Vec<SliceBaseline>,
   126→    /// Overall aggregate across all slices.
   127→    pub overall_pass_rate: f64,
   128→    pub overall_avg_cost: f64,
   129→    pub overall_avg_duration_ms: f64,
   130→    pub overall_avg_iterations: f64,
   131→    pub overall_n_records: usize,
   132→}
   133→```
   134→
   135→### Computation
   136→
   137→`compute_baseline()` groups `TaskMetric` records by `(role, complexity_band)` and computes descriptive statistics for each group:
   138→
   139→```
   140→TaskMetric records
   141→    │
   142→    ▼
   143→Group by (role, complexity_band)
   144→    │
   145→    ├── ("Implementer", "standard") → 156 records
   146→    │       pass_rate: 0.72
   147→    │       avg_cost: $0.83
   148→    │       avg_duration_ms: 45,000
   149→    │       avg_iterations: 1.4
   150→    │
   151→    ├── ("Implementer", "complex") → 48 records
   152→    │       pass_rate: 0.58
   153→    │       avg_cost: $1.52
   154→    │       avg_duration_ms: 120,000
   155→    │       avg_iterations: 2.1
   156→    │
   157→    └── ("Reviewer", "standard") → 89 records
   158→            pass_rate: 0.91
   159→            avg_cost: $0.35
   160→            avg_duration_ms: 12,000
   161→            avg_iterations: 1.1
   162→```
   163→
   164→---
   165→
   166→## Efficiency Events
   167→
   168→The `AgentEfficiencyEvent` provides per-turn cost and quality instrumentation with 20+ fields:
   169→
   170→```rust
   171→pub struct AgentEfficiencyEvent {
   172→    // ── Identity ──────────────────────────────
   173→    pub agent_id: String,
   174→    pub role: String,
   175→    pub backend: String,
   176→    pub model: String,
   177→    pub plan_id: String,
   178→    pub task_id: String,
   179→
   180→    // ── Token accounting ──────────────────────
   181→    pub input_tokens: u64,
   182→    pub output_tokens: u64,
   183→    pub reasoning_tokens: u64,
   184→    pub cache_read_tokens: u64,
   185→    pub cache_write_tokens: u64,
   186→
   187→    // ── Cost accounting ───────────────────────
   188→    pub cost_usd: f64,
   189→    pub cost_usd_without_cache: f64,
   190→
   191→    // ── Prompt composition ────────────────────
   192→    pub prompt_sections: Vec<PromptSectionMeta>,
   193→    pub total_prompt_tokens: u64,
   194→    pub system_prompt_tokens: u64,
   195→
   196→    // ── Tool utilization ──────────────────────
   197→    pub tools_available: u32,
   198→    pub tools_used: u32,
   199→    pub tool_calls: Vec<ToolCallMeta>,
   200→
   201→    // ── Timing ────────────────────────────────
   202→    pub wall_time_ms: u64,
   203→    pub time_to_first_token_ms: u64,
   204→    pub was_warm_start: bool,
   205→}
   206→```
   207→
   208→### PromptSectionMeta
   209→
   210→Attributes token budget consumption to individual prompt sections:
   211→
   212→```rust
   213→pub struct PromptSectionMeta {
   214→    /// Section name (e.g. "prd2", "workspace_map", "playbook_hits").
   215→    pub name: String,
   216→    /// Tokens consumed in the final prompt.
   217→    pub tokens: u64,
   218→    /// Composer-assigned priority (0 = highest).
   219→    pub priority: u8,
   220→    /// Whether this section was truncated due to budget pressure.
   221→    pub was_truncated: bool,
   222→    /// Whether this section was dropped entirely.
   223→    pub was_dropped: bool,
   224→}
   225→```
   226→
   227→### ToolCallMeta
   228→
   229→Per-tool-call instrumentation:
   230→
   231→```rust
   232→pub struct ToolCallMeta {
   233→    /// Tool name (e.g. "Read", "Write", "Bash").
   234→    pub tool_name: String,
   235→    /// Wall-clock duration in milliseconds.
   236→    pub duration_ms: u64,
   237→    /// Tokens in the tool result.
   238→    pub result_tokens: u64,
   239→    /// Whether the call succeeded.
   240→    pub succeeded: bool,
   241→}
   242→```
   243→
   244→---
   245→
   246→## Prompt Efficiency Grading
   247→
   248→The efficiency module provides A-D letter grading for prompt assembly:
   249→
   250→```rust
   251→pub enum Grade {
   252→    A,  // ≥ 0.8 — excellent efficiency
   253→    B,  // ≥ 0.6 — good efficiency
   254→    C,  // ≥ 0.4 — fair efficiency
   255→    D,  // < 0.4 — poor efficiency
   256→}
   257→```
   258→
   259→The `PromptEfficiencyScore` evaluates:
   260→
   261→1. **Section utilization** — what fraction of included sections contributed to the successful outcome?
   262→2. **Token efficiency** — output tokens / input tokens (higher = more productive per token spent).
   263→3. **Cache hit rate** — cache_read_tokens / input_tokens (higher = better cache utilization).
   264→4. **Tool utilization** — tools_used / tools_available (higher = more tools leveraged).
   265→
   266→These four components are weighted and combined into a composite score:
   267→
   268→```
   269→efficiency = 0.30 × section_utilization
   270→           + 0.30 × token_efficiency
   271→           + 0.20 × cache_hit_rate
   272→           + 0.20 × tool_utilization
   273→```
   274→
   275→### Role Cost Profiles
   276→
   277→The `RoleCostProfile` aggregates cost data per agent role:
   278→
   279→```rust
   280→pub struct RoleCostProfile {
   281→    pub role: String,
   282→    pub total_cost_usd: f64,
   283→    pub avg_cost_per_turn: f64,
   284→    pub avg_cost_per_success: f64,
   285→    pub total_turns: u64,
   286→    pub total_successes: u64,
   287→    pub avg_input_tokens: f64,
   288→    pub avg_output_tokens: f64,
   289→    pub avg_cache_hit_rate: f64,
   290→}
   291→```
   292→
   293→These profiles answer operational questions: "Which role is most expensive?" "Does the warm pool save money?" "Which prompt sections drove the cost?"
   294→
   295→---
   296→
   297→## Four Key Metrics
   298→
   299→From the legacy design (mori-agents/07-self-improvement.md), four metrics drive self-improvement:
   300→
   301→| Metric | Definition | Baseline Target |
   302→|--------|-----------|-----------------|
   303→| **First-attempt pass rate** | % of tasks passing gates on first try | > 60% |
   304→| **Iterations per plan** | Average iterations to complete a plan | < 2.0 |
   305→| **Cost per plan** | Total USD spent per plan | Decreasing trend |
   306→| **Prompt tokens per spawn** | Input tokens for the initial agent prompt | < 50K |
   307→
   308→These metrics are computed from the `TaskMetric` stream and surfaced via the `compute_headlines()` function:
   309→
   310→```rust
   311→pub struct Headlines {
   312→    pub total_tasks: usize,
   313→    pub passed_tasks: usize,
   314→    pub pass_rate: f64,
   315→    pub total_cost_usd: f64,
   316→    pub avg_cost_per_task: f64,
   317→    pub avg_iterations: f64,
   318→    pub avg_duration_ms: f64,
   319→}
   320→```
   321→
   322→---

   325→
   326→| Artifact | Format | Path |
   327→|----------|--------|------|

   329→| Efficiency events | JSONL | `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl` |
   330→
   331→Both files are append-only. The `MetricsWriter` batches records in memory and flushes periodically for efficiency.

1→# Naming Map and Glossary
     2→
     3→> **Abstract:** This document provides the authoritative naming map for all Roko
     4→> terminology, mapping legacy names to their current equivalents and defining every
     5→> Roko-specific term, abbreviation, and architectural concept used across the PRD suite.
     6→> When any other document disagrees with a definition here, this glossary is authoritative.
     7→> This serves as a quick-reference companion to the full architecture documentation.
     8→
     9→
    10→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## 1. Project and Framework Names
    15→
    16→The Roko project has undergone several naming iterations. The following table is the
    17→canonical old-to-new mapping. All documentation uses the new names exclusively (with
    18→parenthetical notes when quoting legacy sources).
    19→
    20→| Old Name | New Name | Notes |
    21→|---|---|---|
    22→| Bardo | **Roko** | Overall framework/project name. "Bardo" was the umbrella name for the original ecosystem. |
    23→| Mori | **Roko Orchestrator** | Build/coding orchestration. Often just "orchestrator." The original Mori was a 108K-LOC TypeScript/Rust application for coding agent orchestration. |
    24→| Golem / Golems | **Agent / Agents** | The autonomous entity. "Agent" is the generic term; "Roko agent" when disambiguation is needed. The framework is Roko; individual entities are agents. |
    25→| Grimoire | **Neuro** / `roko-neuro` / **NeuroStore** | Knowledge management subsystem. Persists insights, heuristics, warnings with tier-based decay. |
    26→| Styx | **Agent Mesh** / **Mesh** | P2P relay and permissioned subnets for inter-agent communication and knowledge sharing. |
    27→| GNOS (token) | **KORAI** (mainnet) / **DAEJI** (testnet) | Token names. KORAI is the mainnet token on the Korai chain. DAEJI is the testnet equivalent on the Daeji testnet. |
    28→| Korai | **Korai** | Dedicated EVM chain for agent coordination. Mainnet. |
    29→| Daeji | **Daeji** | Testnet for the Korai chain. |
    30→| Clade | **Collective** / **Mesh** | Groups of cooperating agents. **Never use "fleet"** — this was an incorrect interim rename. |
    31→| Signal (code) | **Engram** (docs) | The universal data type. In Rust code it is `Signal` (`roko-core`). In PRD docs it is "Engram." These are **the same concept** — see note below. |
    32→
    33→> **Signal ↔ Engram:** The Rust codebase uses `Signal` as the struct name for the
    34→> universal datum (defined in `crates/roko-core/src/signal.rs`). The PRD documentation
    35→> calls the same concept an **Engram**, emphasizing its neuroscience roots (Semon 1904).
    36→> Whenever you see `Signal` in code, read it as "Engram." Whenever you see "Engram" in
    37→> docs, the corresponding Rust type is `Signal`. The six verb traits (`Substrate`,
    38→> `Scorer`, `Gate`, `Router`, `Composer`, `Policy`) all operate on `Signal` / Engram.
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→## 2. Configuration Files
    43→
    44→| Old | New |
    45→|---|---|
    46→| `golem.toml` | `roko.toml` |
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## 3. Crate Names
    51→
    52→| Old Crate | New Crate | Notes |
    53→|---|---|---|
    54→| All `golem-*` | `roko-*` | Mechanical rename. |
    55→| All `bardo-*` | `roko-*` | Mechanical rename. Note: `bardo-runtime` and `bardo-primitives` still use old names in the current codebase. Rename is planned. |
    56→| `mori-index` | `roko-index` | Code parsing, symbol graphs, HDC fingerprints. |
    57→| `mori-context` | `roko-compose` + `roko-index` | Context features moved to `roko-compose`; code intelligence moved to `roko-index`. |
    58→| `mori-mcp` | `roko-mcp-{stdio,github,slack,scripts}` | Split into per-transport MCP server crates. |
    59→| `bardo-terminal` | `roko-cli` | The terminal UI is in `roko-cli`, with a separate TUI scaffold. |
    60→| `roko-golem` | **DISSOLVED** | See Crate Dissolution section below. |
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→## 4. Crate Dissolution: `roko-golem`
    65→
    66→The `roko-golem` crate has been dissolved. Its subsystems are redistributed to standalone
    67→crates following the composability principle: any subsystem can pipe to any other through
    68→Engrams and the six Synapse traits. No umbrella crate is needed.
    69→
    70→| Subsystem | Original Location | New Location | Notes |
    71→|---|---|---|---|
    72→| Daimon (972 lines, fully implemented) | `roko-golem/daimon.rs` | `roko-daimon` | Full PAD vector implementation, behavioral states, somatic markers. |
    73→| Dreams (43 lines, placeholder) | `roko-golem/dreams.rs` | `roko-dreams` | Placeholder deleted; `roko-dreams` is the expanded implementation. |
    74→| Grimoire (44 lines, placeholder) | `roko-golem/grimoire.rs` | `roko-neuro` | Placeholder deleted; `roko-neuro` is the replacement with tier-based knowledge management. |
    75→| Chain Witness (43 lines, placeholder) | `roko-golem/chain_witness.rs` | `roko-chain` as `chain_witness` module | Moved. |
    76→| Mortality (44 lines, placeholder) | `roko-golem/mortality.rs` | **DELETED ENTIRELY** | No mortality system in the new architecture. Resource constraints (budget, confidence, time) replace mortality clocks. |
    77→| Hypnagogia (42 lines, placeholder) | `roko-golem/hypnagogia.rs` | `roko-dreams` as `hypnagogia` module | Moved to the Dreams crate. |
    78→| `ScaffoldEngine` trait | `roko-golem/lib.rs` | **DELETED** | Each subsystem defines its own trait. No umbrella needed. |
    79→| `GolemScaffold` aggregator | `roko-golem/lib.rs` | **DELETED** | Composition happens at the application layer via configuration. |
    80→
    81→After dissolution, `roko-golem` is removed from workspace members in `Cargo.toml`.
    82→
    83→**Composability principle**: Any subsystem can pipe to any other. Daimon emits Engrams →
    84→Neuro stores them. Dreams reads from Neuro → produces new Engrams. Chain posts Engrams
    85→on-chain. Everything flows through the six Synapse traits.
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## 5. Core Types
    90→
    91→| Old Name | New Name | Notes |
    92→|---|---|---|
    93→| `Signal` (as architectural noun) | **Engram** | The canonical architectural term. Used in all documentation. |
    94→| `Signal` (as Rust type name) | `Signal` (for now) | The Rust type is still named `Signal` in the current codebase. Rename to `Engram` is Tier 0D in the implementation plan. Documentation uses "Engram" with a note about the current code name. |
    95→| `SignalBuilder` | **EngramBuilder** | Builder pattern for Engram construction. Code currently uses `SignalBuilder`. |
    96→| `signal.rs` | `engram.rs` | Source file rename. |
    97→| "1 noun, 6 verbs" | **Synapse Architecture** | Architecture branding. The six traits are the "synapses" through which Engrams flow. |
    98→
    99→---

   112→
   113→| Token | Chain | Notes |

   115→| **KORAI** | Korai (mainnet) | 1% annual demurrage. Replaces GNOS. |
   116→| **DAEJI** | Daeji (testnet) | Testnet equivalent of KORAI. |
   117→
   118→When a legacy document mentions "GNOS token," the correct new name is "KORAI token" (or

   123→
   124→## 8. Subsystem Names — Kept Unchanged
   125→
   126→These names are preserved from the original architecture with no rename needed:
   127→
   128→| Name | What It Is |
   129→|---|---|
   130→| **Mirage** / **mirage-rs** | In-process EVM simulator for transaction testing. |
   131→| **Heartbeat** | The agent's cognitive loop — one complete cycle of perceive → evaluate → attend → integrate → act → verify → persist → adapt → meta-cognize. |
   132→| **CoALA** | Cognitive Architecture for Language Agents (Sumers et al. 2023). The theoretical framework underlying the universal cognitive loop. |
   133→| **Pheromone system** | Stigmergic coordination: agents leave decaying signals in shared substrate for indirect communication (Grassé 1959, Parunak et al. 2007). |
   134→| **Sleepwalker** | Reduced-capability sleep mode for agents during low-activity periods. |
   135→| **Oneirography** | Recording and analysis of Dreams cycle outputs. |
   136→| **Hypnagogia** | The transitional state between waking and sleeping cognition, implemented as a creative hypothesis generator. |
   137→| **ALMA** | Three-layer affect model (Gebhard 2005) informing the Daimon's emotional processing. |
   138→| **Somatic markers** | Damasio's somatic marker hypothesis (Damasio 1994): emotional signals from past experience that bias decision-making. |
   139→| **Library of Babel** | Cross-collective knowledge sharing — named after Borges' infinite library concept. |
   140→| **Bazaar** | Commerce primitives for agent-to-agent economic interaction. |
   141→| **MPP** | Machine Payment Protocol — protocol for agent-initiated micropayments. |
   142→| **Testament** | Knowledge transfer between agents (repurposed from "death inheritance" to "knowledge export/import"). |
   143→| **Portal** | Interface concept for user interaction surfaces. |
   144→| **Spectre** | The procedurally generated creature visualization — a dot-cloud entity whose body encodes cognitive state. |
   145→| **ROSEDUST** | Dark-only design language (rose on void-black). The visual identity system for all Roko interfaces. |
   146→

   148→
   149→## 9. New Names (Not in Legacy Sources)
   150→
   151→These terms are introduced in the new architecture and do not appear in legacy documents:
   152→
   153→| Term | Definition |
   154→|---|---|
   155→| **Engram** | The core data type — a content-addressed, scored, decaying, lineage-tracked unit of cognition. Replaces "Signal" as the architectural noun. |
   156→| **Synapse Architecture** | The 6-trait composition model. Replaces "1 noun, 6 verbs." |
   157→| **Five Layers** | Runtime (L0) / Framework (L1) / Scaffold (L2) / Harness (L3) / Orchestration (L4). |
   158→| **Cognitive Cross-Cuts** | Neuro / Daimon / Dreams — subsystems injected across multiple layers. |
   159→| **C-Factor** | Collective intelligence ratio metric (Woolley et al. 2010, Science 330). |
   160→| **C-Score** | Composite optimization metric for collective performance. |
   161→| **Three Cognitive Speeds** | Gamma (~5-15s reactive) / Theta (~75s reflective) / Delta (hours consolidation). |
   162→| **16 T0 Probes** | Zero-LLM diagnostic probes that suppress ~80% of ticks to T0 (no inference call). |
   163→| **VCG Attention Auction** | Vickrey-Clarke-Groves mechanism for truthful context budget allocation. |
   164→| **Somatic Landscape** | k-d tree over 8D strategy space with 15% contrarian retrieval for diversity. |
   165→| **Hypnagogia Engine** | Creative hypothesis generator: Thalamic Gate + Executive Loosener + Dali Interrupt + Homuncular Observer. |
   166→| **Cognitive Kernel Primitives** | Namespaces, cognitive signals, scheduling, syscalls — the "operating system" layer. |
   167→| **Korai Passport** | ERC-721 soulbound agent identity on the Korai chain. |
   168→| **Spore / Sparrow** | Job market protocols for agent task discovery and claiming. |
   169→| **ISFR** | Intersubjective Fact Registry — decentralized fact verification. |
   170→| **Valhalla** | Privacy layer using TEE, PSI (Private Set Intersection), and ZK proofs. |
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→## 10. Glossary of Architectural Terms
   175→
   176→### A
   177→
   178→| Term | Definition |
   179→|---|---|
   180→| **A2A** | Agent-to-Agent protocol. Google's open standard for inter-agent communication. |
   181→| **A2UI** | Agent-to-User-Interface. Agents generate their own UI components in ROSEDUST. |
   182→| **Active Inference** | Framework from neuroscience (Friston 2010): self-organizing systems minimize prediction error through perception and action. Roko uses this as a conceptual framework for attention and tier routing. |
   183→| **ADAS** | Automated Design of Agentic Systems (Hu et al. ICLR 2025). Meta-agent that searches the space of agent architectures. |
   184→| **Adaptive Clock** | The scheduler that manages three cognitive speeds (Gamma/Theta/Delta) and adjusts cadence based on affect state and task characteristics. |
   185→| **Agent Mesh** | P2P communication and knowledge sharing between Roko agents. Replaces "Styx." |
   186→| **Attestation** | Cryptographic proof of Engram origin — Ed25519 signature with optional chain attestation. |
   187→
   188→### B
   189→
   190→| Term | Definition |
   191→|---|---|
   192→| **Body** | The typed payload of an Engram. Variants: Empty (marker), Text (UTF-8), Json (structured), Bytes (binary). |
   193→| **Budget** | Resource constraints for Composer operations: max_tokens, max_signals, max_bytes, max_wall_ms. |
   194→
   195→### C
   196→
   197→| Term | Definition |
   198→|---|---|
   199→| **C-Factor** | Collective intelligence ratio: `C-Factor = Collective Performance / Sum(Individual Performances)`. Values > 1.0 indicate superlinear intelligence. (Woolley et al. 2010) |
   200→| **C-Score** | Composite optimization metric: `gate_pass×0.3 + cost_efficiency×0.2 + speed×0.15 + first_try_rate×0.25 + knowledge_growth×0.1`. |
   201→| **CascadeRouter** | Multi-stage model routing: confidence threshold → contextual bandit (LinUCB) → cost-aware selection. Persisted to `.roko/learn/cascade-router.json`. |
   202→| **Cognitive Loop** | The 9-step universal loop: PERCEIVE → EVALUATE → ATTEND → INTEGRATE → ACT → VERIFY → PERSIST → ADAPT → META-COGNIZE. |
   203→| **Collective** | A group of cooperating agents. Replaces "Clade." |
   204→| **Composer** | Synapse trait. Combines multiple Engrams into one new Engram under Budget constraints. Synchronous. Takes `&dyn Scorer`. |
   205→| **ContentHash** | 32-byte BLAKE3 digest identifying an Engram. Computed from kind + body + author + tags. Score and decay are excluded. |
   206→| **Context** | The shared runtime environment passed to every trait method. Carries time (`now_ms`), goal, session, and extension attributes. |
   207→
   208→### D
   209→
   210→| Term | Definition |
   211→|---|---|
   212→| **Daimon** | Motivation and focus subsystem (`roko-daimon`). Maintains PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) vectors, six behavioral states, and somatic markers. The agent's self-model. |
   213→| **Decay** | Time-based weight reduction for Engrams. Variants: None (permanent), HalfLife (exponential), Ttl (hard cutoff), Ebbinghaus (psychological forgetting curve). |
   214→| **Delta** | Consolidation cognitive speed. Hours timescale. Dreams: replay, synthesis, pruning. Knowledge tier promotion. |
   215→| **Dreams** | Offline learning subsystem (`roko-dreams`). Three-phase cycle: NREM replay + REM imagination + integration staging. |
   216→| **Dual-Process** | Kahneman's System 1/System 2 mapped to Roko: T0 (direct tool call, no LLM) / T1 (fast model) / T2 (full model, deep reasoning). |
   217→
   218→### E
   219→
   220→| Term | Definition |
   221→|---|---|
   222→| **EFE** | Expected Free Energy. `G(π) = −Pragmatic Value − Epistemic Value`. Drives tier routing without hyperparameters. |
   223→| **Engram** | The universal data type of Roko. Content-addressed (BLAKE3), scored (7-axis), decaying, lineage-tracked. Every piece of information in the system is an Engram. Currently named `Signal` in code; rename is Tier 0D. |
   224→| **EngramBuilder** | Builder pattern for constructing Engrams. Currently named `SignalBuilder` in code. |
   225→| **Episode** | A recorded sequence of agent actions and their outcomes. Stored as Engrams with `Kind::Episode`. |
   226→| **EvoSkills** | Self-evolving skill libraries via adversarial surrogate verification (Zhao et al. 2024). |
   227→
   228→### F-G
   229→
   230→| Term | Definition |
   231→|---|---|
   232→| **Forensic AI** | Content-addressed causal replay — the ability to trace any decision back to its inputs via lineage chains. Regulatory pre-compliance moat. |
   233→| **Gamma** | Reactive cognitive speed. ~5-15 second timescale. One complete loop tick: tool calls, LLM inference, verification. |
   234→| **Gate** | Synapse trait. Verifies an Engram against ground truth, producing a Verdict. Asynchronous. The bridge to external reality. |
   235→
   236→### H-K
   237→
   238→| Term | Definition |
   239→|---|---|
   240→| **HDC** | Hyperdimensional Computing. 10,240-bit vectors using XOR bind, majority bundle, Hamming similarity. Used for semantic similarity in knowledge retrieval. (Kanerva 2009, Cognitive Computation 1(2); Plate 2003; Frady et al. 2018) |
   241→| **Hypnagogia** | The creative hypothesis generator in `roko-dreams`. Four components: Thalamic Gate (stimulus filter), Executive Loosener (constraint relaxation), Dali Interrupt (capture mechanism), Homuncular Observer (coherence filter). |
   242→| **Kind** | The semantic type of an Engram. `#[non_exhaustive]` enum with variants for agent runtime, verification, tasks, context, routing, memory, observability, chain participation, and `Custom(String)` for extensions. |
   243→| **KORAI** | Mainnet token on the Korai chain. 1% annual demurrage. Replaces "GNOS." |
   244→| **Korai** | Dedicated EVM chain for agent coordination. |
   245→
   246→### L-N
   247→
   248→| Term | Definition |
   249→|---|---|
   250→| **Lineage** | A vector of ContentHashes tracking the parent Engrams from which a new Engram was derived. Forms an audit DAG. |
   251→| **Neuro** | Knowledge management subsystem (`roko-neuro`). Six knowledge types (Insight, Heuristic, Warning, CausalLink, StrategyFragment, AntiKnowledge). Four tiers (Transient, Working, Consolidated, Persistent). HDC encoding for similarity. |
   252→| **NeuroStore** | The storage backend for the Neuro subsystem. |
   253→
   254→### O-P
   255→
   256→| Term | Definition |
   257→|---|---|
   258→| **Outcome** | Feedback about a prior Router selection. Carries success/failure, reward, cost, and latency. Used by Routers to learn via bandit algorithms. |
   259→| **Pheromone** | A stigmergic signal with time-based decay. Three types: THREAT (2h half-life), OPPORTUNITY (4h), WISDOM (24h). Agents communicate indirectly by reading and writing pheromones to shared Substrates. |
   260→| **Policy** | Synapse trait. Watches Engram streams and emits new Engrams in response (interventions, reactions). Synchronous. Batch input. |
   261→| **Provenance** | Who produced an Engram and how trustworthy they are. Fields: author (String), trust ([0,1]), tainted (bool), session (Option). |
   262→
   263→### Q-R
   264→
   265→| Term | Definition |
   266→|---|---|
   267→| **Query** | Filter specification for Substrate lookups. Fields: kinds, author, session, since_ms, until_ms, min_weight, tags, limit. |
   268→| **ROSEDUST** | Dark-only design system. Rose on void-black. The visual identity for all Roko interfaces. |
   269→| **Router** | Synapse trait. Selects one Engram from candidates. Has `feedback()` method for learning from outcomes. Synchronous. |
   270→
   271→### S
   272→
   273→| Term | Definition |
   274→|---|---|
   275→| **Score** | Multi-dimensional quality assessment of an Engram. 4 stable axes: confidence [0,1], novelty [0,1], utility [0,∞), reputation [0,∞). 3 extended axes (planned): precision [0,1], salience [0,1], coherence [0,1]. Effective score formula: `confidence × (1 + novelty) × (1 + utility) × reputation`. |
   276→| **Scorer** | Synapse trait. Rates an Engram along multiple axes, producing a Score. Synchronous. Pure function of (Engram, Context). |
   277→| **Selection** | Output of a Router: the chosen Engram's ContentHash, confidence, router name, and optional reasoning. |
   278→| **Spectre** | Procedurally generated dot-cloud creature per agent. Body encodes cognitive state; eyes encode emotion; clarity encodes prediction accuracy. |
   279→| **Substrate** | Synapse trait. Stores and queries Engrams. Asynchronous. Multiple backends: MemorySubstrate (testing), FileSubstrate (JSONL persistence), HdcSubstrate (semantic search), ChainSubstrate (on-chain state). |
   280→| **Synapse Architecture** | The compositional model underlying Roko: one noun (Engram) + six verb traits (Substrate, Scorer, Gate, Router, Composer, Policy). |
   281→
   282→### T-V
   283→
   284→| Term | Definition |
   285→|---|---|
   286→| **T0 / T1 / T2** | Inference tiers. T0: no LLM call (direct tool call, ~80% of ticks). T1: fast model, shallow reasoning (~15%). T2: full model, deep reasoning (~5%). Routing emerges from active inference (EFE). |
   287→| **Theta** | Reflective cognitive speed. ~75 second timescale. Summarize recent work, update Daimon state, check predictions. |
   288→| **TickOutcome** | The result of one `loop_tick` invocation. Contains: candidates_examined, composed Engram, Verdict, emitted Engrams, written ContentHashes. |
   289→| **Verdict** | Output of a Gate. Contains: passed (bool), reason (String), gate name, score [0,1], optional detail, optional TestCount, optional error_digest, duration_ms. |

   293→## 11. Naming Application Rules
   294→
   295→1. **When quoting legacy sources verbatim**: Keep the old name in the quote but add a
   296→   parenthetical: "(formerly Grimoire, now Neuro)."
   297→2. **When paraphrasing**: Use the new name directly.
   298→3. **Code samples**: Use new crate names (`roko-primitives`, `roko-runtime`, etc.) even if
   299→   the current Rust code still uses old names. Note the current name in a comment.
   300→4. **Struct/type names**: Say "Engram" in prose. In Rust code snippets, use `Signal` and
   301→   add a comment like `// will be renamed to Engram in Tier 0D`.
   302→5. **File paths**: All `bardo-*` → `roko-*`, all `mori-*` → `roko-*`.
   303→6. **Never say**: "Golem SDK" → say "Agent SDK" or "Roko SDK".
   304→7. **Never say**: "Mori + Golem" → say "Roko framework with coding and chain domain plugins."

1→# Regression Detection
     2→
     3→> **Crate:** `roko-learn` · **Module:** `regression.rs`
     4→> **Wiring:** `LearningRuntime::record_completed_run()` → regression check
     5→> **Cross-references:** [06-task-metrics-and-baselines](06-task-metrics-and-baselines.md), [14-stability-mechanisms](14-stability-mechanisms.md), [15-collective-calibration-31x](15-collective-calibration-31x.md)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Purpose
    13→
    14→The regression detector answers a critical question: "Did this configuration change make things worse?" It compares a fresh batch of `TaskMetric` records against a previously computed `Baseline` and fires alerts when key indicators breach configurable thresholds. This closes the feedback loop between system changes (prompt modifications, model routing updates, playbook rule changes) and their observable impact on task outcomes.
    15→
    16→Without regression detection, the learning system could silently degrade: a bandit might converge on a model that worked well last week but performs poorly after a provider update, or a playbook rule might be promoted despite introducing regressions in edge cases. The regression detector surfaces these degradations as structured alerts that trigger investigation or automatic rollback.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Threshold Configuration
    21→
    22→```rust
    23→pub struct RegressionThresholds {
    24→    /// Maximum allowed drop in first-attempt pass rate (default: 0.15 = 15%).
    25→    pub pass_rate_drop: f64,
    26→    /// Maximum allowed increase in average cost (default: 0.20 = 20%).
    27→    pub cost_increase: f64,
    28→    /// Maximum allowed increase in average duration (default: 0.30 = 30%).
    29→    pub duration_increase: f64,
    30→    /// Maximum allowed increase in average iterations (default: 0.25 = 25%).
    31→    pub iterations_increase: f64,
    32→    /// Minimum records needed before detection fires (default: 5).
    33→    pub min_records: usize,
    34→}
    35→```
    36→
    37→### Default Thresholds
    38→
    39→| Metric | Threshold | Severity | Rationale |
    40→|--------|-----------|----------|-----------|
    41→| Pass rate drop | > 15% | **Alert** | Direct impact on task completion |
    42→| Cost increase | > 20% | **Alert** | Budget impact |
    43→| Duration increase | > 30% | Warning | May be acceptable for higher quality |
    44→| Iterations increase | > 25% | Warning | More iterations may reflect harder tasks |
    45→
    46→The asymmetry between Alert and Warning severities reflects priority: pass rate and cost regressions are immediate blockers that demand investigation, while duration and iteration increases may be acceptable tradeoffs (e.g., a harder task mix this week).
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## Detection Algorithm
    51→
    52→The `detect_regressions()` function compares current metrics against a baseline:
    53→
    54→```
    55→Baseline (from historical TaskMetric records)
    56→    │
    57→    ▼
    58→Current batch (recent N task metrics)
    59→    │
    60→    ▼
    61→For each (role, complexity_band) slice:
    62→    │
    63→    ├── Pass rate regression:
    64→    │     change = (baseline.pass_rate - current.pass_rate) / baseline.pass_rate
    65→    │     if change > pass_rate_drop → Alert
    66→    │     if change < -pass_rate_drop → Improvement
    67→    │
    68→    ├── Cost regression:
    69→    │     change = (current.avg_cost - baseline.avg_cost) / baseline.avg_cost
    70→    │     if change > cost_increase → Alert
    71→    │     if change < -cost_increase → Improvement
    72→    │
    73→    ├── Duration regression:
    74→    │     change = (current.avg_duration - baseline.avg_duration) / baseline.avg_duration
    75→    │     if change > duration_increase → Warning
    76→    │     if change < -duration_increase → Improvement
    77→    │
    78→    └── Iterations regression:
    79→          change = (current.avg_iterations - baseline.avg_iterations) / baseline.avg_iterations
    80→          if change > iterations_increase → Warning
    81→          if change < -iterations_increase → Improvement
    82→```
    83→
    84→### Per-Slice Analysis
    85→
    86→Regressions are detected per `(role, complexity_band)` slice, not just in aggregate. This prevents a scenario where a severe regression in "Implementer/complex" tasks is masked by improvements in "Reviewer/standard" tasks. Each slice that has enough records (≥ `min_records`) is analyzed independently.
    87→
    88→---
    89→
    90→## Alert Schema
    91→
    92→```rust
    93→pub enum AlertSeverity {
    94→    Alert,        // Key metric breached (pass rate, cost)
    95→    Warning,      // Secondary metric breached (duration, iterations)
    96→    Improvement,  // Metric improved relative to baseline
    97→}
    98→
    99→pub struct RegressionAlert {
   100→    /// Which metric regressed (e.g. "pass_rate", "cost").
   101→    pub metric_name: String,
   102→    /// Severity.
   103→    pub severity: AlertSeverity,
   104→    /// Baseline value.
   105→    pub baseline_value: f64,
   106→    /// Current (observed) value.
   107→    pub current_value: f64,
   108→    /// Fractional change (positive = worsened).
   109→    pub change_fraction: f64,
   110→    /// The threshold that was breached.
   111→    pub threshold: f64,
   112→    /// Human-readable description.
   113→    pub description: String,
   114→    /// Optional (role, complexity) slice. None = overall.
   115→    pub slice: Option<(String, String)>,
   116→}
   117→```
   118→
   119→### RegressionReport
   120→
   121→```rust
   122→pub struct RegressionReport {
   123→    /// All alerts (breaches and improvements).
   124→    pub alerts: Vec<RegressionAlert>,
   125→    /// Whether any alert has Alert severity.
   126→    pub has_regressions: bool,
   127→    /// Whether the current data set has enough records.
   128→    pub sufficient_data: bool,
   129→    /// Number of current records analyzed.
   130→    pub current_records: usize,
   131→    /// Number of baseline records.
   132→    pub baseline_records: usize,
   133→}
   134→```
   135→
   136→The report provides convenience methods:
   137→- `regressions()` — filter to Alert-severity items only.
   138→- `warnings()` — filter to Warning-severity items only.
   139→
   140→---
   141→
   142→## LearningRuntime Integration
   143→
   144→The regression detector runs as part of `LearningRuntime::record_completed_run()` when a `RegressionConfig` is configured:
   145→
   146→```rust
   147→pub struct RegressionConfig {
   148→    pub thresholds: RegressionThresholds,
   149→    /// Number of latest metrics used as the "current" sample.
   150→    pub current_window: usize,  // default: 20
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→The runtime:
   155→1. Reads all `TaskMetric` records from `.roko/learn/task-metrics.jsonl`.
   156→2. Splits into baseline (all records except the latest `current_window`) and current (latest `current_window` records).
   157→3. Computes baselines for both sets.
   158→4. Calls `detect_regressions(baseline, current, thresholds)`.
   159→5. If `report.has_regressions`, logs the alerts and optionally triggers corrective actions.
   160→
   161→### Current Window
   162→
   163→The `current_window` parameter (default: 20) determines how many recent metrics are treated as "current" for comparison against the baseline. This value balances:
   164→- **Too small** (< 10): noisy, a single outlier can trigger false alerts.
   165→- **Too large** (> 50): sluggish, a real regression takes many tasks to surface.
   166→- **20** provides a reasonable tradeoff: enough data for statistical stability, but responsive enough to catch regressions within a single plan execution.
   167→
   168→---
   169→
   170→## C-Factor Regression
   171→
   172→In addition to per-metric regression detection, the C-Factor module provides its own regression check over the composite C-Factor score:
   173→
   174→```rust
   175→pub struct CFactorRegression {
   176→    pub current_snapshot_at: DateTime<Utc>,
   177→    pub window_start: DateTime<Utc>,
   178→    pub window_end: DateTime<Utc>,
   179→    pub sample_count: usize,
   180→    // ... delta analysis fields
   181→}
   182→```
   183→
   184→C-Factor regression detects systemic decline: when the composite score drops against a trailing history window, it indicates that the system as a whole is performing worse, even if individual metrics haven't breached their thresholds. This catches subtle multi-dimensional regressions where pass rate drops slightly, cost rises slightly, and speed decreases slightly — none individually alarming, but collectively significant.
   185→
   186→See [15-collective-calibration-31x](15-collective-calibration-31x.md) for the C-Factor computation.
   187→
   188→---
   189→
   190→## Adaptive Gate Thresholds
   191→
   192→Regression detection interacts with the adaptive gate threshold system. Gate thresholds (pass/fail criteria for compile, test, lint, diff gates) are adjusted via EMA (Exponential Moving Average) based on historical pass rates:
   193→
   194→```
   195→Gate threshold = EMA(pass_rates, alpha=0.1)
   196→```
   197→
   198→When the regression detector fires a pass_rate Alert, it signals that thresholds may need recalibration. The adaptive threshold system can then tighten thresholds (require higher quality) or loosen them (accept the current performance level) based on operational priorities.
   199→
   200→See [04-verification](../04-verification/INDEX.md) for the gate pipeline and adaptive threshold mechanism.
   201→
   202→---
   203→
   204→## Practical Example
   205→
   206→Consider a system running 200 tasks over 3 days. After task 150, a prompt template change was deployed.
   207→
   208→### Baseline (tasks 1-130)
   209→
   210→```
   211→Overall:
   212→    pass_rate: 0.72
   213→    avg_cost: $0.83
   214→    avg_duration_ms: 45,000
   215→    avg_iterations: 1.4
   216→
   217→Slice (Implementer, standard):
   218→    pass_rate: 0.75
   219→    avg_cost: $0.78
   220→    avg_duration_ms: 42,000
   221→    avg_iterations: 1.3
   222→```
   223→
   224→### Current window (tasks 151-170, after template change)
   225→
   226→```
   227→Overall:
   228→    pass_rate: 0.55     ← dropped
   229→    avg_cost: $1.12     ← increased
   230→    avg_duration_ms: 52,000
   231→    avg_iterations: 1.9
   232→
   233→Slice (Implementer, standard):
   234→    pass_rate: 0.50     ← dropped significantly
   235→    avg_cost: $1.05     ← increased
   236→    avg_duration_ms: 48,000
   237→    avg_iterations: 2.0
   238→```
   239→
   240→### Regression Report
   241→
   242→```
   243→ALERT: pass_rate regression in (Implementer, standard)
   244→    Baseline: 0.75, Current: 0.50
   245→    Change: -33.3% (threshold: 15%)
   246→    "Pass rate dropped from 75% to 50% for Implementer/standard tasks"
   247→
   248→ALERT: cost regression in (Implementer, standard)
   249→    Baseline: $0.78, Current: $1.05
   250→    Change: +34.6% (threshold: 20%)
   251→    "Average cost increased from $0.78 to $1.05 for Implementer/standard tasks"
   252→
   253→WARNING: iterations regression in (Implementer, standard)
   254→    Baseline: 1.3, Current: 2.0
   255→    Change: +53.8% (threshold: 25%)
   256→    "Average iterations increased from 1.3 to 2.0 for Implementer/standard tasks"
   257→```
   258→
   259→### Corrective Action
   260→
   261→The regression report identifies that the prompt template change degraded Implementer/standard tasks. The operator can:
   262→1. **Revert**: Roll back the template change.
   263→2. **Investigate**: Examine which specific tasks failed and why.
   264→3. **Adjust**: Modify the template to address the failure pattern while preserving improvements in other slices.
   265→
   266→Without regression detection, this degradation would be invisible — the system would continue spending more money for worse results.
   267→
   268→---
   269→
   270→## False Positive Management
   271→
   272→Regression detection can produce false positives when:
   273→- The task mix shifts (harder tasks → lower pass rate, not a regression).
   274→- A single expensive task dominates the cost average.
   275→- The current window is too small to be statistically stable.
   276→
   277→Mitigation strategies:
   278→1. **min_records threshold**: Don't fire alerts with fewer than 5 records per slice.
   279→2. **Per-slice analysis**: Detect whether the regression is slice-specific or systemic.
   280→3. **Improvement tracking**: Report improvements alongside regressions to provide context.
   281→4. **Config hash correlation**: If a specific config change correlates with the regression, flag it as the likely cause.
   282→

1→# Cost Normalization
     2→
     3→> **Crate:** `roko-learn` · **Modules:** `costs_db.rs`, `costs_log.rs`
     4→> **Persistence:** `.roko/learn/costs.jsonl`
     5→> **Implementation plan:** `modelrouting/09-cost-normalization.md` (tasks 2H.01–2H.10)
     6→> **Cross-references:** [04-cascade-router](04-cascade-router.md), [06-task-metrics-and-baselines](06-task-metrics-and-baselines.md), [09-provider-health-circuit-breaker](09-provider-health-circuit-breaker.md)
     7→
     8→
     9→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Purpose
    14→
    15→Cost normalization provides a consistent framework for comparing model costs across providers, pricing tiers, and token types. Raw cost data from providers is heterogeneous: some charge per input token, some per output token, some per request; cache hits reduce costs differently; reasoning tokens may have distinct pricing. The cost normalization layer transforms this into a single comparable metric — blended cost per million tokens — that the cascade router and budget guardrails can use for routing decisions.
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## CostRecord Schema
    20→
    21→```rust
    22→pub struct CostRecord {
    23→    /// Timestamp of the cost observation.
    24→    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    25→    /// Model slug (e.g. "claude-sonnet-4-20250514").
    26→    pub model: String,
    27→    /// Provider identifier (e.g. "anthropic", "openrouter").
    28→    pub provider: String,
    29→    /// Agent role.
    30→    pub role: String,
    31→    /// Plan identifier.
    32→    pub plan_id: String,
    33→    /// Task identifier.
    34→    pub task_id: String,
    35→    /// Complexity band ("fast", "standard", "complex").
    36→    pub complexity_band: String,
    37→    /// Input tokens consumed.
    38→    pub input_tokens: u64,
    39→    /// Output tokens generated.
    40→    pub output_tokens: u64,
    41→    /// Total cost in USD.
    42→    pub cost_usd: f64,
    43→    /// Duration in milliseconds.
    44→    pub duration_ms: u64,
    45→    /// Whether the task succeeded.
    46→    pub success: bool,
    47→    /// Session identifier for grouping.
    48→    pub session_id: String,
    49→}
    50→```
    51→
    52→### CostSummary
    53→
    54→Aggregate view over a collection of `CostRecord`s:
    55→
    56→```rust
    57→pub struct CostSummary {
    58→    /// Total records summarized.
    59→    pub total_records: usize,
    60→    /// Total cost across all records.
    61→    pub total_cost_usd: f64,
    62→    /// Average cost per record.
    63→    pub avg_cost_usd: f64,
    64→    /// Total input tokens.
    65→    pub total_input_tokens: u64,
    66→    /// Total output tokens.
    67→    pub total_output_tokens: u64,
    68→    /// Average cost per successful task.
    69→    pub avg_cost_per_success: f64,
    70→    /// Number of successful records.
    71→    pub success_count: usize,
    72→}
    73→```
    74→
    75→---
    76→
    77→## Blended Cost Formula
    78→
    79→The blended cost per million tokens uses a 3:1 input-to-output weighting ratio, following the Artificial Analysis methodology:
    80→
    81→```
    82→blended_cost_per_m = (3 × input_price_per_m + 1 × output_price_per_m) / 4
    83→```
    84→
    85→### Why 3:1?
    86→
    87→The 3:1 ratio reflects the typical token mix in agent workloads: agents read more than they write. In Roko's measured workloads, the median input-to-output ratio is approximately 3:1 (for every output token generated, the agent consumes ~3 input tokens in context, prior conversation, and tool results). Using this ratio makes the blended cost metric correspond to actual expenditure patterns.
    88→
    89→### Token-Type Normalization
    90→
    91→Not all input tokens cost the same:
    92→
    93→| Token Type | Typical Pricing | Normalization |
    94→|------------|----------------|---------------|
    95→| Fresh input tokens | Full input price | 1.0× |
    96→| Cache read tokens | 10-90% discount | Weighted by actual cache price |
    97→| Cache write tokens | Usually same as input | 1.0× |
    98→| Reasoning/thinking tokens | Usually same as output | Counted as output |
    99→| System prompt tokens | Full input price | 1.0× (but often cached) |
   100→
   101→The `AgentEfficiencyEvent` captures both `cost_usd` (actual cost after cache discounts) and `cost_usd_without_cache` (hypothetical full-price cost), enabling analysis of cache savings.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## CostTable Design
   106→
   107→The CostTable (from implementation plan 2H.01–2H.04) structures per-model pricing:
   108→
   109→```rust
   110→pub struct ModelPricing {
   111→    /// Model slug.
   112→    pub model: String,
   113→    /// Provider.
   114→    pub provider: String,
   115→    /// Input price per million tokens.
   116→    pub input_price_per_m: f64,
   117→    /// Output price per million tokens.
   118→    pub output_price_per_m: f64,
   119→    /// Cache read price per million tokens (if different).
   120→    pub cache_read_price_per_m: Option<f64>,
   121→    /// Blended cost per million tokens (3:1 ratio).
   122→    pub blended_cost_per_m: f64,
   123→}
   124→```
   125→
   126→The CostTable is loaded from configuration and updated periodically as providers change pricing. It provides the cost signal that the cascade router uses in its cost penalty computation.
   127→
   128→---
   129→
   130→## Budget Guardrails
   131→
   132→The budget guardrail system (from implementation plan 2H.05–2H.10) enforces multi-level spending limits:
   133→
   134→```rust
   135→pub struct BudgetGuardrail {
   136→    /// Per-task cost limit in USD.
   137→    pub per_task_limit: f64,
   138→    /// Per-session cost limit in USD.
   139→    pub per_session_limit: f64,
   140→    /// Per-day cost limit in USD.
   141→    pub per_day_limit: f64,
   142→}
   143→
   144→pub enum BudgetAction {
   145→    /// Continue with current model.
   146→    Continue,
   147→    /// Downgrade to a cheaper model (triggered at 80% of limit).
   148→    Downgrade,
   149→    /// Block the request (triggered at 95% of limit).
   150→    Block,
   151→    /// Hard stop — no further requests (triggered at 100% of limit).
   152→    HardStop,
   153→}
   154→```
   155→
   156→### Escalation Thresholds
   157→
   158→| Level | % of Limit | Action | Rationale |
   159→|-------|------------|--------|-----------|
   160→| Normal | < 80% | Continue | Full freedom |
   161→| Warn | 80% | Downgrade | Route to cheaper model |
   162→| Block | 95% | Block | Reject new requests |
   163→| Hard stop | 100% | HardStop | Terminate session |
   164→
   165→The downgrade action at 80% is a soft intervention: instead of failing the task, the router automatically selects a cheaper model. This preserves task completion while controlling costs. The 95% block prevents any new requests from starting, and the 100% hard stop terminates the session entirely.
   166→
   167→### Multi-Level Enforcement
   168→
   169→Budget limits are enforced at three levels simultaneously:
   170→
   171→1. **Per-task** — prevents a single task from consuming disproportionate resources. A task that exceeds its budget is downgraded or blocked before reaching the session limit.
   172→2. **Per-session** — prevents a session (typically one plan execution) from exceeding its allocation. This catches scenarios where many cheap tasks collectively exceed budget.
   173→3. **Per-day** — absolute daily spending cap. This protects against runaway automation that might execute many plans in a day.
   174→
   175→```
   176→Incoming request
   177→    │
   178→    ▼
   179→Check per-task budget
   180→    │ if task_cost ≥ 80% of per_task_limit → Downgrade
   181→    │ if task_cost ≥ 95% of per_task_limit → Block
   182→    │
   183→    ▼
   184→Check per-session budget
   185→    │ if session_cost ≥ 80% of per_session_limit → Downgrade
   186→    │ if session_cost ≥ 95% of per_session_limit → Block
   187→    │
   188→    ▼
   189→Check per-day budget
   190→    │ if day_cost ≥ 80% of per_day_limit → Downgrade
   191→    │ if day_cost ≥ 100% of per_day_limit → HardStop
   192→    │
   193→    ▼
   194→Route to selected model (or cheaper alternative if Downgrade)
   195→```
   196→
   197→---
   198→
   199→## CostsLog: Append-Only Persistence
   200→
   201→The `CostsLog` provides durable, file-backed persistence for `CostRecord` values:
   202→
   203→```rust
   204→pub struct CostsLog {
   205→    path: PathBuf,
   206→    fsync: bool,
   207→}
   208→```
   209→
   210→Key operations:
   211→- `CostsLog::at(path)` — construct a log at `path` with fsync enabled.
   212→- `CostsLog::open_creating(path)` — create parent directories and construct a log.
   213→- `CostsLog::append(record)` — append one `CostRecord` as a JSON line with optional fsync.
   214→- `CostsLog::append_all(records)` — batch append with a single open/close cycle.
   215→- `CostsLog::read_all(path)` — read all records, tolerant of corrupt lines.
   216→
   217→The `without_fsync()` builder method disables per-append fsync for test environments where crash safety is not needed.
   218→
   219→### Relationship to CostsDb
   220→
   221→`CostsDb` is the in-memory cost database used for real-time queries. `CostsLog` is its durable companion: each completed call is appended to the log, and the log is replayed on startup to reconstruct the in-memory database. This separation keeps the hot path fast (in-memory lookups) while maintaining durability (append-only JSONL).
   222→
   223→---
   224→
   225→## Cost-to-Routing Feedback Loop
   226→
   227→Cost data feeds back into routing through two paths:
   228→
   229→1. **Direct cost penalty in cascade router** — the confidence stage subtracts a cost penalty from each model's score, biasing toward cheaper models when pass rates are similar.
   230→2. **Budget guardrail enforcement** — when budget limits approach, the guardrail system forces the router to select cheaper models or block requests entirely.
   231→
   232→This creates cybernetic feedback loop 6 (Cost→Routing) from [13-8-missing-feedback-loops](13-8-missing-feedback-loops.md): higher costs trigger routing changes that reduce costs, which relaxes the budget pressure, which may allow routing back to better (more expensive) models.
   233→
   234→---
   235→

   240→- **[09-provider-health-circuit-breaker](09-provider-health-circuit-breaker.md)** — Provider health affects cost indirectly: unhealthy providers cause retries that increase total cost.
   241→- **[13-8-missing-feedback-loops](13-8-missing-feedback-loops.md)** — Loop 6 (Cost→Routing) describes the cost-to-routing feedback path.
   242→- **[10-pareto-frontier-pruning](10-pareto-frontier-pruning.md)** — Pareto pruning uses cost_per_success as one of its optimization axes.
