1→# The Engram Data Type
     2→
     3→> **Abstract:** The Engram is the universal datum of the Roko system. Every event, every
     4→> piece of data, every agent output, every gate verdict, every knowledge entry, every
     5→> prediction, every tool trace — is an Engram. Engrams are content-addressed (BLAKE3),
     6→> scored (7-axis appraisal), decaying (four decay models), lineage-tracked (audit DAG),
     7→> and provenance-stamped (author + trust + taint). This document specifies the Engram
     8→> struct in full detail, explains each field, describes the content-addressing scheme,
     9→> and shows how Engrams flow through the Synapse Architecture.
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    13→
    14→> **Code ↔ Docs Naming:** In the Rust codebase (`roko-core`), the universal data type is
    15→> called **`Signal`** (struct `Signal` in `crates/roko-core/src/signal.rs`). In these PRD
    16→> documents, the same concept is called **Engram**. The names are synonymous — "Engram"
    17→> emphasizes the cognitive/neuroscience framing, while "Signal" is the Rust identifier.
    18→> When reading code, `Signal` = Engram. When reading docs, Engram = `Signal`. The six
    19→> verb traits (`Substrate`, `Scorer`, `Gate`, `Router`, `Composer`, `Policy`) operate on
    20→> `Signal` in code and on "Engram" in documentation.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## 1. Why One Universal Type
    25→
    26→Classical software architectures use many types: tasks, events, messages, requests, responses,
    27→records, logs. Each type has its own schema, its own storage, its own lifecycle. Adding a new
    28→capability means adding a new type, a new store, a new API.
    29→
    30→Roko takes a different approach. There is exactly one data type — the **Engram** — and six
    31→traits that operate on it. This design choice has three consequences:
    32→
    33→1. **Universal composability**: Any Scorer can score any Engram. Any Substrate can store any
    34→   Engram. Any Gate can verify any Engram. Components compose freely because they all speak
    35→   the same language.
    36→
    37→2. **Full audit trails**: Every Engram carries lineage — the ContentHashes of the parent
    38→   Engrams it was derived from. This forms a directed acyclic graph (DAG) that can be
    39→   traversed to explain any decision: why was this model chosen? What context was used? What
    40→   gate verdict was rendered? Follow the lineage.
    41→
    42→3. **Temporal dynamics**: Every Engram decays. Knowledge fades. Pheromone signals expire.
    43→   Context becomes stale. The system's "memory" is not a static database — it is a living
    44→   substrate where information has weight that changes over time.
    45→
    46→The name "Engram" comes from neuroscience: a hypothetical means by which memories are stored
    47→as biophysical changes in the brain (Semon 1904; Lashley 1950; Tonegawa et al. 2015, Science
    48→348(6238)). In Roko, an Engram is the digital equivalent — a content-addressed unit of
    49→cognition that persists, decays, and can be retrieved by similarity or query.
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→## 2. The Engram Struct
    54→
    55→The target Engram struct (the architectural specification). The current Rust type is named
    56→`Signal`; rename to `Engram` is Tier 0D in the implementation plan.
    57→
    58→```rust
    59→/// The universal datum of the Roko system.
    60→///
    61→/// See crate-level docs for the architectural role of Engram.
    62→///
    63→/// # Identity
    64→///
    65→/// An Engram's identity is its ContentHash, computed from its kind, body,
    66→/// author, and tags. Score and decay are excluded — they can change
    67→/// without changing identity.
    68→#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    69→pub struct Signal { // will be renamed to Engram in Tier 0D
    70→    /// Content-addressed identity (computed from kind + body + author + tags).
    71→    pub id: ContentHash,
    72→    /// What kind of Engram this is.
    73→    pub kind: Kind,
    74→    /// The Engram's payload.
    75→    pub body: Body,
    76→    /// Unix milliseconds when this Engram was first emitted.
    77→    pub created_at_ms: i64,
    78→    /// How this Engram's weight decays over time.
    79→    pub decay: Decay,
    80→    /// Producer attribution and trust.
    81→    pub provenance: Provenance,
    82→    /// Quality score at emission time (may be recomputed by Scorers).
    83→    pub score: Score,
    84→    /// ContentHashes of Engrams this derived from (forms a DAG for auditing
    85→    /// and autocatalytic metrics).
    86→    pub lineage: Vec<ContentHash>,
    87→    /// Arbitrary string metadata (ordered for stable hashing).
    88→    pub tags: BTreeMap<String, String>,
    89→}
    90→```
    91→
    92→The extended Engram adds an attestation field (specified but not yet in the current code):
    93→
    94→```rust
    95→pub struct Engram {
    96→    pub id: ContentHash,              // BLAKE3(kind + body + author + tags)
    97→    pub kind: Kind,                   // semantic type (#[non_exhaustive] enum + Custom(String))
    98→    pub body: Body,                   // payload (text, JSON, binary)
    99→    pub tags: BTreeMap<String, String>, // ordered metadata (included in hash)
   100→    pub created_at_ms: i64,           // Unix milliseconds
   101→    pub decay: Decay,                 // None | HalfLife | Ttl | Ebbinghaus
   102→    pub score: Score,                 // 7-axis appraisal
   103→    pub lineage: Vec<ContentHash>,    // parent Engrams (audit DAG)
   104→    pub provenance: Provenance,       // author, model fingerprint, taint chain
   105→    pub attestation: Option<Attestation>, // cryptographic proof of origin
   106→}
   107→```
   108→
   109→Each field is specified in detail in the sections that follow.
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→## 3. ContentHash — Identity Through Content
   114→
   115→An Engram's identity is its `ContentHash`: a 32-byte BLAKE3 digest computed from the
   116→Engram's canonical encoding. Two Engrams with identical content have identical hashes.
   117→
   118→```rust
   119→/// A 32-byte content-addressed identifier (BLAKE3 digest).
   120→///
   121→/// Two Engrams with identical canonical encoding share the same ContentHash.
   122→/// The hash is computed over the Engram's body and its identity fields, but
   123→/// not its score or decay — those can change without changing identity.
   124→#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   125→pub struct ContentHash(pub [u8; 32]);
   126→
   127→impl ContentHash {
   128→    /// Compute a content hash from arbitrary bytes.
   129→    #[must_use]
   130→    pub fn of(bytes: &[u8]) -> Self {
   131→        Self(*blake3::hash(bytes).as_bytes())
   132→    }
   133→
   134→    /// Hex-encoded representation (64 chars).
   135→    #[must_use]
   136→    pub fn to_hex(&self) -> String {
   137→        use std::fmt::Write;
   138→        let mut s = String::with_capacity(64);
   139→        for byte in self.0 {
   140→            let _ = write!(s, "{byte:02x}");
   141→        }
   142→        s
   143→    }
   144→
   145→    /// Short form for logs/display (first 8 hex chars).
   146→    #[must_use]
   147→    pub fn short(&self) -> String {
   148→        format!(
   149→            "{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}",
   150→            self.0[0], self.0[1], self.0[2], self.0[3]
   151→        )
   152→    }
   153→
   154→    /// Parse a hex string into a ContentHash. Returns None for malformed input.
   155→    #[must_use]
   156→    pub fn from_hex(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
   157→        if s.len() != 64 {
   158→            return None;
   159→        }
   160→        let mut bytes = [0u8; 32];
   161→        for (i, chunk) in s.as_bytes().chunks_exact(2).enumerate() {
   162→            let hi = hex_digit(chunk[0])?;
   163→            let lo = hex_digit(chunk[1])?;
   164→            bytes[i] = (hi << 4) | lo;
   165→        }
   166→        Some(Self(bytes))
   167→    }
   168→}
   169→```
   170→
   171→### 3.1 Why BLAKE3
   172→
   173→BLAKE3 was chosen over SHA-256 for three reasons:
   174→
   175→1. **Speed**: BLAKE3 is ~5× faster than SHA-256 on modern hardware due to its tree-based
   176→   structure and SIMD optimizations.
   177→2. **Streaming**: BLAKE3 supports incremental hashing, which matters when Engrams contain
   178→   large payloads (file contents, compiled artifacts).
   179→3. **Keyed mode**: BLAKE3 supports keyed hashing for MAC computation, useful for attestation.
   180→
   181→### 3.2 What Is Hashed (Identity Fields)
   182→
   183→The content hash covers:
   184→
   185→- `kind` — the semantic type (via `kind.as_str().as_bytes()`)
   186→- `body` — the payload (via `body.canonical_bytes()`)
   187→- `provenance.author` — who produced this Engram
   188→- `provenance.tainted` — whether the Engram contains untrusted data
   189→- `lineage` — the parent ContentHashes
   190→- `tags` — all key-value pairs in sorted order (BTreeMap guarantees sort order)
   191→
   192→### 3.3 What Is NOT Hashed (Mutable Fields)
   193→
   194→The content hash **excludes**:
   195→
   196→- `score` — Scores can be recomputed by different Scorers in different contexts without
   197→  changing what the Engram fundamentally is.
   198→- `decay` — Decay can be adjusted (e.g., promoted from HalfLife to None for persistent
   199→  knowledge) without changing identity.
   200→- `created_at_ms` — Creation time is metadata, not content. Two Engrams with identical
   201→  content produced at different times should deduplicate.
   202→
   203→This design means that `Substrate.put()` is **idempotent**: re-putting the same Engram
   204→produces the same ContentHash and is a no-op in the Substrate.
   205→
   206→### 3.4 Hash Computation (Shipping Code)
   207→
   208→The exact hash computation from the current codebase
   209→(`roko-core/src/signal.rs:content_hash()`):
   210→
   211→```rust
   212→pub fn content_hash(&self) -> ContentHash {
   213→    let mut hasher = blake3::Hasher::new();
   214→    hasher.update(self.kind.as_str().as_bytes());
   215→    hasher.update(b"|");
   216→    hasher.update(&self.body.canonical_bytes());
   217→    hasher.update(b"|");
   218→    hasher.update(self.provenance.author.as_bytes());
   219→    hasher.update(b"|");
   220→    hasher.update(&[u8::from(self.provenance.tainted)]);
   221→    hasher.update(b"|");
   222→    for h in &self.lineage {
   223→        hasher.update(&h.0);
   224→    }
   225→    hasher.update(b"|");
   226→    for (k, v) in &self.tags {
   227→        hasher.update(k.as_bytes());
   228→        hasher.update(b"=");
   229→        hasher.update(v.as_bytes());
   230→        hasher.update(b";");
   231→    }
   232→    ContentHash(*hasher.finalize().as_bytes())
   233→}
   234→```
   235→
   236→Fields are separated by `|` delimiters. Tags use `key=value;` format with semicolons. The
   237→BTreeMap guarantees lexicographic key order, making the hash deterministic regardless of
   238→insertion order.
   239→
   240→---
   241→
   242→## 4. Kind — Semantic Type
   243→
   244→The `Kind` enum tells consumers how to interpret an Engram's body. Kinds are grouped by
   245→architectural concern and are `#[non_exhaustive]` with a `Custom(String)` escape hatch for
   246→extensions.
   247→
   248→```rust
   249→#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   250→#[non_exhaustive]
   251→#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
   252→pub enum Kind {
   253→    // ─── Agent runtime ───────────────────────────────
   254→    ProcessSpawn,         // A process was spawned
   255→    ProcessExit,          // A process exited
   256→    AgentMessage,         // Message chunk from an agent's stream
   257→    AgentOutput,          // Raw stdout/stderr from an agent
   258→    TokenUsage,           // Token usage report from an LLM call
   259→    ApprovalRequested,    // Agent requested approval for destructive op
   260→
   261→    // ─── Verification ────────────────────────────────
   262→    GateVerdict,          // Gate passed or failed a check
   263→    TestResult,           // Test suite run result
   264→    CompileDiagnostic,    // Compile error or warning
   265→
   266→    // ─── Tasks & plans ───────────────────────────────
   267→    Task,                 // Task description (input to agent)
   268→    Plan,                 // Plan (collection of tasks with deps)
   269→    PlanPhase,            // Plan transitioned phases
   270→
   271→    // ─── Context assembly ────────────────────────────
   272→    PromptSection,        // Single section within an assembled prompt
   273→    ContextPack,          // Curated bundle of context for an agent
   274→    Prompt,               // Fully-assembled prompt ready for LLM
   275→
   276→    // ─── Routing & learning ──────────────────────────
   277→    RouterChoice,         // Router decision (e.g., "use Claude")
   278→    RouterFeedback,       // Feedback about a prior router choice
   279→
   280→    // ─── Memory ──────────────────────────────────────
   281→    Episode,              // Logged episode of an agent run
   282→    PlaybookRule,         // Playbook rule extracted from patterns
   283→    Skill,                // Learned reusable procedure
   284→
   285→    // ─── Observability ───────────────────────────────
   286→    Metric,               // Scalar measurement
   287→    ExperimentResult,     // A/B test outcome
   288→    ToolInvocation,       // Tool invocation record
   289→    ToolHealthDegraded,   // Tool health below threshold
   290→
   291→    // ─── Chain participation ─────────────────────────
   292→    Insight,              // Shared knowledge
   293→    Pheromone,            // Stigmergic signal (threat/opportunity/wisdom)
   294→    Bounty,               // Bounty available for claiming
   295→    Transaction,          // On-chain transaction
   296→    Service,              // Service offering (OaaS marketplace)
   297→    Prediction,           // Prediction claim (predictive foraging)
   298→
   299→    // ─── Extension ───────────────────────────────────
   300→    Custom(String),       // Extension kind (reverse-DNS: "com.example.my_kind")
   301→}
   302→```
   303→
   304→### 4.1 Extensibility
   305→
   306→The `Kind` enum is `#[non_exhaustive]`, meaning new variants can be added without breaking
   307→downstream implementations. For domain-specific kinds that don't belong in the core enum,
   308→use `Kind::Custom("com.example.widget".into())` with reverse-DNS prefixes to avoid
   309→collisions.
   310→
   311→This design allows the system to grow without modifying `roko-core`. A chain domain plugin
   312→can define `Kind::Custom("chain.pheromone.threat")` for domain-specific pheromone types
   313→without touching the kernel.
   314→
   315→### 4.2 Kind as Dispatch Key
   316→
   317→Kinds serve as the switchyard for dispatch throughout the system:
   318→
   319→- A Gate might only verify Engrams of kind `GateVerdict` or `TestResult`
   320→- A Composer might only combine `PromptSection` Engrams into a `Prompt`
   321→- A Policy might watch for `ToolHealthDegraded` Engrams and emit circuit-breaker responses
   322→- A Router might select among `RouterChoice` candidates based on historical feedback
   323→
   324→---
   325→
   326→## 5. Body — Typed Payload
   327→
   328→The `Body` enum carries the Engram's actual content. It is tagged so consumers can determine
   329→the payload format at runtime before decoding.
   330→
   331→```rust
   332→#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   333→#[serde(tag = "format", content = "data", rename_all = "snake_case")]
   334→pub enum Body {
   335→    /// Empty — the Engram is purely a marker (kind and tags carry meaning).
   336→    Empty,
   337→    /// UTF-8 text (logs, prompts, messages).
   338→    Text(String),
   339→    /// Structured JSON value.
   340→    Json(serde_json::Value),
   341→    /// Raw bytes (binary artifacts, compressed data).
   342→    Bytes(Vec<u8>),
   343→}
   344→```
   345→
   346→### 5.1 Body Variants
   347→
   348→| Variant | When Used | Size Method |
   349→|---|---|---|
   350→| `Empty` | Marker Engrams where the Kind and tags carry all meaning (e.g., `ProcessSpawn`) | `byte_size() → 0` |
   351→| `Text(String)` | Logs, prompts, messages, natural-language content | `byte_size() → string.len()` |
   352→| `Json(Value)` | Structured data: tool call parameters, gate results, configuration | `byte_size() → json.to_string().len()` |
   353→| `Bytes(Vec<u8>)` | Binary artifacts, compressed data, HDC vectors | `byte_size() → bytes.len()` |
   354→
   355→### 5.2 Canonical Encoding
   356→
   357→For content hashing, the Body produces a canonical byte representation via
   358→`body.canonical_bytes()`. This uses JSON serialization to ensure stability across serde
   359→versions:
   360→
   361→```rust
   362→pub fn canonical_bytes(&self) -> Vec<u8> {
   363→    serde_json::to_vec(self).unwrap_or_default()
   364→}
   365→```
   366→
   367→### 5.3 Typed Decoding
   368→
   369→Bodies support typed decoding via `as_json<T>()`, `as_text()`, and `as_bytes()`. Each
   370→returns a `Result` that errors if the body variant doesn't match:
   371→
   372→```rust
   373→// Decode a JSON body into a typed value
   374→let body = Body::from_json(&my_struct)?;
   375→let decoded: MyStruct = body.as_json()?;
   376→
   377→// Get text content
   378→let text_body = Body::text("hello");
   379→let text: &str = text_body.as_text()?;
   380→```
   381→
   382→---
   383→
   384→## 6. Lineage — The Audit DAG
   385→
   386→The `lineage` field is a vector of `ContentHash`es identifying the parent Engrams from which
   387→this Engram was derived. This forms a directed acyclic graph (DAG) that enables:
   388→
   389→- **Causal replay**: Trace any decision back to its inputs by following lineage chains.
   390→- **Impact analysis**: Find all Engrams that depend on a given input.
   391→- **Autocatalytic metrics**: Measure how many downstream Engrams an input catalyzed.
   392→- **Forensic audit**: Reconstruct the complete chain of reasoning for any output.
   393→
   394→### 6.1 Lineage Construction
   395→
   396→When a Gate verifies an Engram and produces a Verdict, the Verdict Engram's lineage includes
   397→the input Engram's ContentHash. When a Composer combines multiple Engrams, the composed
   398→output's lineage includes all input ContentHashes. The `derive()` method on Engram
   399→automates this:
   400→
   401→```rust
   402→impl Signal { // will be renamed to Engram
   403→    /// Emit a derived Engram — new kind/body, but tracks this Engram as lineage.
   404→    pub fn derive(&self, kind: Kind, body: Body) -> SignalBuilder {
   405→        SignalBuilder::new(kind)
   406→            .body(body)
   407→            .lineage([self.id])
   408→            .provenance(Provenance::agent("derived"))
   409→    }
   410→}
   411→```
   412→
   413→### 6.2 DAG Traversal
   414→
   415→The lineage DAG can be traversed by querying the Substrate for each parent ContentHash:
   416→
   417→```rust
   418→// Trace an Engram's full lineage
   419→async fn trace_lineage(
   420→    substrate: &dyn Substrate,
   421→    engram: &Signal,   // will be renamed to Engram
   422→) -> Vec<Signal> {     // will be renamed to Engram
   423→    let mut ancestors = Vec::new();
   424→    let mut queue: VecDeque<ContentHash> = engram.lineage.iter().copied().collect();
   425→    let mut seen = HashSet::new();
   426→
   427→    while let Some(id) = queue.pop_front() {
   428→        if !seen.insert(id) {
   429→            continue; // already visited
   430→        }
   431→        if let Ok(Some(parent)) = substrate.get(&id).await {
   432→            queue.extend(parent.lineage.iter().copied());
   433→            ancestors.push(parent);
   434→        }
   435→    }
   436→    ancestors
   437→}
   438→```
   439→
   440→---
   441→
   442→## 7. The Builder Pattern
   443→
   444→Engrams are constructed using the builder pattern, which provides sensible defaults:
   445→
   446→```rust
   447→pub struct SignalBuilder { // will be renamed to EngramBuilder
   448→    kind: Kind,
   449→    body: Body,
   450→    created_at_ms: Option<i64>,
   451→    decay: Decay,
   452→    provenance: Provenance,
   453→    score: Score,
   454→    lineage: Vec<ContentHash>,
   455→    tags: BTreeMap<String, String>,
   456→}
   457→```
   458→
   459→### 7.1 Defaults
   460→
   461→| Field | Default | Rationale |
   462→|---|---|---|
   463→| `body` | `Body::Empty` | Marker Engrams are common |
   464→| `created_at_ms` | Current wall-clock time | Most Engrams are created "now" |
   465→| `decay` | `Decay::None` | Conservative — explicit opt-in to decay |
   466→| `provenance` | `Provenance::default()` (trusted, author="roko") | Internal Engrams are trusted |
   467→| `score` | `Score::NEUTRAL` (confidence=0.5, novelty=0, utility=0, reputation=1) | Neutral until scored |
   468→| `lineage` | Empty vec | No parents unless specified |
   469→| `tags` | Empty BTreeMap | No metadata unless specified |
   470→
   471→### 7.2 Usage Examples
   472→
   473→```rust
   474→use roko_core::{Signal, Kind, Body, Decay, Provenance, Score};
   475→
   476→// Simple task Engram
   477→let task = Signal::builder(Kind::Task)
   478→    .body(Body::text("implement login"))
   479→    .tag("priority", "high")
   480→    .build();
   481→
   482→// Pheromone with decay
   483→let pheromone = Signal::builder(Kind::Pheromone)
   484→    .body(Body::text("high gas prices detected"))
   485→    .decay(Decay::HalfLife { half_life_ms: 14_400_000 }) // 4 hours
   486→    .provenance(Provenance::agent("chain_monitor"))
   487→    .tag("type", "opportunity")
   488→    .build();
   489→
   490→// Gate verdict derived from a task Engram
   491→let verdict = task.derive(Kind::GateVerdict, Body::text("compilation passed"))
   492→    .score(Score::new(1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 1.0))
   493→    .build();
   494→// verdict.lineage == [task.id]
   495→
   496→// Episode with JSON body
   497→let episode_data = serde_json::json!({
   498→    "action": "file_edit",
   499→    "path": "src/main.rs",
   500→    "outcome": "success",
   501→    "tokens_used": 1500
   502→});
   503→let episode = Signal::builder(Kind::Episode)
   504→    .body(Body::Json(episode_data))
   505→    .decay(Decay::HalfLife { half_life_ms: 604_800_000 }) // 7 days
   506→    .tag("run", "42")
   507→    .build();
   508→```
   509→
   510→### 7.3 Finalization
   511→
   512→The `build()` method computes the content hash and freezes the Engram:
   513→
   514→```rust
   515→pub fn build(self) -> Signal { // will be renamed to Engram
   516→    let created_at_ms = self.created_at_ms.unwrap_or_else(current_time_ms);
   517→    let mut signal = Signal {
   518→        id: ContentHash([0; 32]), // placeholder
   519→        kind: self.kind,
   520→        body: self.body,
   521→        created_at_ms,
   522→        decay: self.decay,
   523→        provenance: self.provenance,
   524→        score: self.score,
   525→        lineage: self.lineage,
   526→        tags: self.tags,
   527→    };
   528→    signal.id = signal.content_hash();
   529→    signal
   530→}
   531→```
   532→
   533→---
   534→
   535→## 8. Effective Weight
   536→
   537→An Engram's effective weight at a given time combines its score and its decay:
   538→
   539→```rust
   540→/// The effective weight of this Engram at the given current time.
   541→/// Combines score × decay.
   542→pub fn weight_at(&self, now_ms: i64) -> f32 {
   543→    let age = now_ms - self.created_at_ms;
   544→    self.score.effective() * self.decay.apply(age)
   545→}
   546→```
   547→
   548→This is the primary ordering criterion for Substrate queries with `min_weight` filters.
   549→An Engram that was highly scored at creation but has decayed significantly may fall below
   550→the weight threshold and be excluded from query results — or pruned entirely by
   551→`Substrate.prune()`.
   552→
   553→---
   554→
   555→## 9. Serde and Persistence
   556→
   557→Engrams are fully serializable via serde. The default persistence format is JSONL
   558→(JSON Lines) in the `FileSubstrate` (`roko-fs`), where each line is one Engram:
   559→
   560→```json
   561→{"id":"a1b2c3d4...","kind":"task","body":{"format":"text","data":"implement login"},"created_at_ms":1712345678000,"decay":{"kind":"none"},"provenance":{"author":"roko","trust":1.0,"tainted":false,"session":null},"score":{"confidence":0.5,"novelty":0.0,"utility":0.0,"reputation":1.0},"lineage":[],"tags":{"priority":"high"}}
   562→```
   563→
   564→ContentHashes serialize as hex strings (64 characters). Byte bodies serialize as base64.
   565→This ensures JSONL files are valid UTF-8 throughout.
   566→

   568→
   569→## 10. Engram Properties Summary
   570→
   571→| Property | Value | Implication |
   572→|---|---|---|
   573→| **Content-addressed** | BLAKE3(kind + body + author + tags) | Deduplication, integrity, addressable storage |
   574→| **Scored** | 7-axis (4 stable + 3 extended) | Multi-dimensional quality assessment |
   575→| **Decaying** | 4 models (None, HalfLife, Ttl, Ebbinghaus) | Temporal dynamics, automatic memory management |
   576→| **Lineage-tracked** | Vec<ContentHash> | Audit DAG, causal replay, forensic AI |
   577→| **Provenance-stamped** | author + trust + tainted + session | Taint analysis, audit trails, reputation |
   578→| **Typed payload** | Body enum (Empty, Text, Json, Bytes) | Runtime type checking, canonical encoding |
   579→| **Extensible kind** | `#[non_exhaustive]` + Custom(String) | New capabilities without core changes |
   580→| **Serializable** | serde Serialize + Deserialize | JSONL persistence, network transport |
   581→
   582→---
   583→
   584→## 11. Content-Addressing: Comparison with IPFS CIDs and Git Objects
   585→
   586→Roko's `ContentHash` is a fixed 32-byte BLAKE3 digest. Two other major content-addressed
   587→systems provide instructive comparisons.
   588→
   589→### 11.1 Git Object Model
   590→
   591→Git stores four object types — blobs, trees, commits, and tags — each identified by the
   592→SHA-1/SHA-256 hash of its content. The commit graph is a Merkle-DAG where parent pointers
   593→are content hashes. Roko's `lineage: Vec<ContentHash>` is the direct analog of Git's parent
   594→commit pointers — both form audit DAGs traversable by hash (Merkle 1989).
   595→
   596→### 11.2 IPFS CIDs and Self-Describing Hashes
   597→
   598→IPFS Content Identifiers (CIDs) are **self-describing**: a CIDv1 encodes the hash function,
   599→digest length, and content codec via the Multiformats standard:
   600→
   601→```
   602→CIDv1 = <multibase-prefix><version><multicodec><multihash>
   603→multihash = <hash-function-code><digest-length><digest-bytes>
   604→```
   605→
   606→Roko's `ContentHash` is compact (32 bytes) but not self-describing. A 2-byte multihash
   607→prefix (`0x1e 0x20` = "blake3-256, 32 bytes") would make hashes future-proof and
   608→IPLD-compatible at trivial cost (34 bytes instead of 32).
   609→
   610→### 11.3 IPLD Data Model Layer
   611→
   612→InterPlanetary Linked Data (IPLD) adds a universal data model on top of content addressing
   613→with **DAG-CBOR** (RFC 8949 with deterministic encoding) as the canonical codec. The key
   614→design insight: separate the data model from the codec from the identifier from the transport.
   615→This maximizes composability — a codec change does not invalidate existing CIDs.
   616→
   617→---
   618→
   619→## 12. Binary Serialization: Format Comparison
   620→
   621→| Format | Deterministic | Self-Describing | Zero-Copy | Schema Evo | Best For |
   622→|---|---|---|---|---|---|
   623→| **serde_json** (current) | Yes | Yes | No | Forward | Hash stability, debugging |
   624→| **DAG-CBOR** | Yes (RFC 8949 §4.2) | Yes | No | Forward | IPLD-compatible canonical encoding |
   625→| **postcard** | Yes (documented) | No | No | None | Minimal binary, `no_std` |
   626→| **rkyv** | No (arch-dependent) | No | Yes | None | Bulk HDC vector access |
   627→| **bincode** | No | No | No | None | Fast IPC, not for hashing |
   628→
   629→If compactness becomes important, **DAG-CBOR** would reduce canonical bytes by 30-60% while
   630→maintaining determinism and IPLD compatibility. Keep **rkyv** for zero-copy HDC access only.
   631→
   632→---
   633→
   634→## 13. Schema Evolution for Content-Addressed Types
   635→
   636→### 13.1 The Two-Partition Design
   637→
   638→| Partition | Fields | Hashed? | Evolution |
   639→|---|---|---|---|
   640→| **Identity** | kind, body, author, tainted, lineage, tags | Yes | Adding creates new identity (correct) |
   641→| **Mutable** | score, decay, created_at_ms, attestation | No | Evolves freely |
   642→
   643→This mirrors Protocol Buffers' field number stability rule (Kleppmann 2017): identity fields
   644→are frozen; mutable fields can change without breaking existing hashes.
   645→
   646→### 13.2 CRDT Compatibility
   647→
   648→The lineage DAG is structurally compatible with Merkle-CRDTs (Sanjuán et al. 2020,
   649→arXiv:2004.00107). If two agents independently derive Engrams from the same parent, the
   650→union of their lineage graphs is well-defined by content addressing — deduplication is
   651→automatic. The Score's `utility` and `reputation` axes could be modeled as grow-only CRDT
   652→counters for distributed accumulation.
   653→
   654→---
   655→
   656→## 14. Information-Theoretic Properties
   657→
   658→### 14.1 Complexity Estimation via Compression
   659→
   660→Kolmogorov complexity K(x) — the length of the shortest program outputting x — is
   661→uncomputable (Kolmogorov 1965; Chaitin 1966) but upper-bounded by any compressor's output:
   662→
   663→```
   664→complexity_ratio = len(compress(body)) / len(body)
   665→```
   666→
   667→High ratio (near 1.0) → incompressible → likely novel. Low ratio → highly compressible →
   668→likely redundant. This provides a **substrate-free novelty signal** — no query needed.
   669→
   670→### 14.2 Bayesian Surprise as Novelty
   671→
   672→Itti & Baldi (2005) define Bayesian surprise as `S(data) = KL[P(M|data) || P(M)]`. Engrams
   673→with high Bayesian surprise deserve high novelty scores. Schmidhuber (2010) formalizes this
   674→as "compression progress" — the intrinsic reward from learning to compress data better.
   675→
   676→### 14.3 MDL for the Coherence Axis
   677→
   678→Grünwald's MDL principle (2007) provides a rigorous definition for the planned `coherence`
   679→axis: `coherence = 1.0 - (L(D|M) / L(D|null_model))`, where the model M is the existing
   680→corpus of same-Kind Engrams.
   681→
   682→```rust
   683→/// ComplexityScorer: computes local complexity as a Kolmogorov proxy.
   684→pub struct ComplexityScorer;
   685→
   686→impl Scorer for ComplexityScorer {
   687→    fn score(&self, signal: &Signal, _ctx: &Context) -> Score {
   688→        let raw = signal.body.canonical_bytes();
   689→        if raw.is_empty() { return Score::NEUTRAL; }
   690→        let compressed = zstd::encode_all(&raw[..], 1).unwrap_or_default();
   691→        let ratio = compressed.len() as f32 / raw.len() as f32;
   692→        Score { confidence: 0.5, novelty: ratio.clamp(0.0, 1.0), utility: 0.0, reputation: 1.0 }
   693→    }
   694→    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "complexity_scorer" }
   695→}
   696→```
   697→
   698→---
   699→
   700→## 15. Engram Algebra: VSA Operations
   701→
   702→Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) define algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors
   703→that preserve compositional structure. Extending these to Engrams creates a proper algebra.
   704→
   705→### 15.1 The Three Operations
   706→
   707→| Operation | HDC Implementation | Engram Meaning |
   708→|---|---|---|
   709→| **Bind** (⊗) | XOR of HDC vectors | Associate two Engrams (key-value pair) |
   710→| **Bundle** (⊕) | Majority vote | Create cluster centroid / composite |
   711→| **Permute** (ρ) | Cyclic bit shift | Encode temporal ordering |
   712→
   713→### 15.2 Record Encoding via HDC
   714→
   715→An Engram's Score can be encoded as a single HDC vector for nanosecond-speed approximate
   716→nearest-neighbor queries over score profiles:
   717→
   718→```
   719→score_hdc = BUNDLE([
   720→    BIND(ROLE_CONFIDENCE, QUANTIZE(0.9)),
   721→    BIND(ROLE_NOVELTY, QUANTIZE(0.3)),
   722→    BIND(ROLE_UTILITY, QUANTIZE(5.0)),
   723→    BIND(ROLE_REPUTATION, QUANTIZE(1.2)),
   724→])
   725→```
   726→
   727→### 15.3 Algebraic Properties
   728→
   729→| Property | Bind (XOR) | Bundle (Majority) |
   730→|---|---|---|
   731→| Commutative | Yes | Yes |
   732→| Associative | Yes | Approximately |
   733→| Identity | Zero vector | None |
   734→| Inverse | Self-inverse (a ⊗ a = 0) | None (lossy) |
   735→
   736→Bind forms an **abelian group**; bundle forms a **commutative semigroup**. Together they
   737→provide the algebraic structure for compositional knowledge representation (Plate 2003;
   738→Gayler 2004; Kleyko et al. 2022).
   739→
   740→```rust
   741→impl Signal { // will be renamed to Engram
   742→    /// Bind: create an association between two Engrams (XOR in HDC space).
   743→    pub fn bind(&self, other: &Signal) -> HdcVector {
   744→        self.fingerprint().xor(&other.fingerprint())
   745→    }
   746→
   747→    /// Bundle: create a composite similar to ALL inputs (majority vote).
   748→    pub fn bundle(engrams: &[Signal]) -> HdcVector {
   749→        HdcVector::majority(
   750→            &engrams.iter().map(|e| e.fingerprint()).collect::<Vec<_>>()
   751→        )
   752→    }
   753→
   754→    /// Permute: encode this Engram at a sequence position.
   755→    pub fn at_position(&self, position: usize) -> HdcVector {
   756→        self.fingerprint().rotate(position)
   757→    }
   758→}
   759→```

1→# Provider Health and Circuit Breaker
     2→
     3→> **Crate:** `roko-learn` · **Module:** `provider_health.rs`
     4→> **Wiring:** `ProviderHealthRegistry` → `CascadeRouter::select()` (filters unhealthy providers)
     5→> **Implementation plan:** `modelrouting/08-learning-loops.md` (tasks 2G.01–2G.06)
     6→> **Cross-references:** [04-cascade-router](04-cascade-router.md), [08-cost-normalization](08-cost-normalization.md), [13-8-missing-feedback-loops](13-8-missing-feedback-loops.md)
     7→
     8→
     9→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Purpose
    14→
    15→The provider health module tracks the operational status of each LLM provider and implements a three-state circuit breaker that prevents routing requests to degraded or failing providers. When a provider starts returning errors (rate limits, timeouts, server errors), the circuit breaker opens, diverting traffic to healthy alternatives. After a cooldown period, the circuit breaker transitions to half-open, allowing a single probe request to test recovery before fully restoring traffic.
    16→
    17→This is cybernetic feedback loop 1 (Health→Routing) from the eight missing feedback loops: provider health state directly influences routing decisions in the cascade router.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Three-State Circuit Breaker
    22→
    23→```
    24→                 success
    25→    ┌──────────────────────────┐
    26→    │                          │
    27→    ▼                          │
    28→┌────────┐   failure threshold  ┌────────────┐
    29→│ CLOSED │ ────────────────────►│   OPEN     │
    30→│(normal)│                      │(no traffic)│
    31→└────────┘                      └──────┬─────┘
    32→    ▲                                  │
    33→    │                          cooldown expires
    34→    │         success                  │
    35→    │  ┌──────────────────┐            │
    36→    └──┤    HALF-OPEN     │◄───────────┘
    37→       │(single probe req)│
    38→       └──────────────────┘
    39→              │
    40→              │ failure
    41→              │
    42→              ▼
    43→          OPEN (reset cooldown)
    44→```
    45→
    46→### States
    47→
    48→| State | Behavior | Transition |
    49→|-------|----------|------------|
    50→| **Closed** | Normal operation. All requests are routed. Failures are counted. | → Open: when failure count exceeds threshold within window |
    51→| **Open** | No requests are routed. Traffic is diverted to alternative providers. | → Half-Open: after cooldown period expires |
    52→| **Half-Open** | A single probe request is allowed through. | → Closed: if probe succeeds · → Open: if probe fails (reset cooldown) |
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## Error Classification
    57→
    58→Not all errors are equal. The provider health module classifies errors by type to apply appropriate cooldown policies:
    59→
    60→```rust
    61→pub enum ErrorClass {
    62→    /// HTTP 429 — provider rate limit.
    63→    RateLimit,
    64→    /// HTTP 401/403 — authentication or authorization failure.
    65→    AuthFailure,
    66→    /// Request or response timeout.
    67→    Timeout,
    68→    /// HTTP 5xx — provider server error.
    69→    ServerError,
    70→    /// Content policy violation (filtered response).
    71→    ContentPolicy,
    72→    /// Context window exceeded.
    73→    ContextOverflow,
    74→    /// Unclassified error.
    75→    Unknown,
    76→}
    77→```
    78→
    79→### Error-Specific Cooldowns
    80→
    81→Each error class has a tailored cooldown strategy:
    82→
    83→| Error Class | Cooldown | Rationale |
    84→|-------------|----------|-----------|
    85→| `RateLimit` | 60s (escalating with backoff) | Provider will recover after rate window resets |
    86→| `AuthFailure` | 300s (long) | Requires manual intervention (API key rotation) |
    87→| `Timeout` | 30s | Often transient network issues |
    88→| `ServerError` | 120s | Provider-side issues, variable recovery |
    89→| `ContentPolicy` | 0s (no cooldown, flag only) | Not a provider health issue — content-specific |
    90→| `ContextOverflow` | 0s (no cooldown, route to larger model) | Not a provider issue — task-specific |
    91→| `Unknown` | 60s (conservative default) | Unknown errors get conservative treatment |
    92→
    93→### Failure Records
    94→
    95→Each failure is recorded with its classification:
    96→
    97→```rust
    98→pub struct FailureRecord {
    99→    /// When the failure occurred.
   100→    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
   101→    /// Error classification.
   102→    pub error_class: ErrorClass,
   103→    /// Raw error message (truncated to 256 chars).
   104→    pub message: String,
   105→    /// Model that was being used.
   106→    pub model: String,
   107→}
   108→```
   109→
   110→---
   111→
   112→## ProviderHealth
   113→
   114→Per-provider health state:
   115→
   116→```rust
   117→pub struct ProviderHealth {
   118→    /// Provider identifier.
   119→    pub provider_id: String,
   120→    /// Current circuit breaker state.
   121→    pub state: CircuitState,
   122→    /// Recent failure records (bounded window).
   123→    pub recent_failures: VecDeque<FailureRecord>,
   124→    /// Total failure count since last reset.
   125→    pub failure_count: u64,
   126→    /// Total success count since last reset.
   127→    pub success_count: u64,
   128→    /// When the circuit breaker last opened.
   129→    pub last_opened: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
   130→    /// When the circuit breaker will transition to half-open.
   131→    pub cooldown_until: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
   132→}
   133→```
   134→
   135→### Threshold Configuration
   136→
   137→The circuit breaker opens when:
   138→- **Failure count** exceeds the threshold within the observation window, OR
   139→- **Failure rate** (failures / total requests) exceeds the rate threshold.
   140→
   141→Default values:
   142→- Failure count threshold: 5 failures
   143→- Observation window: 60 seconds
   144→- Failure rate threshold: 50%
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## ProviderHealthRegistry
   149→
   150→The registry manages health state for all providers:
   151→
   152→```rust
   153→pub struct ProviderHealthRegistry {
   154→    providers: Mutex<HashMap<String, ProviderHealth>>,
   155→}
   156→```
   157→
   158→Key operations:
   159→
   160→| Method | What it does |
   161→|--------|-------------|
   162→| `record_success(provider)` | Increment success count. If half-open, transition to closed. |
   163→| `record_failure(provider, error_class)` | Record failure. Check threshold. If exceeded, open circuit. |
   164→| `is_available(provider)` | Returns `true` if circuit is Closed or Half-Open. |
   165→| `available_providers()` | Returns all providers with Closed or Half-Open circuits. |
   166→
   167→### Integration with Cascade Router
   168→
   169→The cascade router calls `is_available()` before scoring each candidate model:
   170→
   171→```
   172→CascadeRouter::select(context)
   173→    │
   174→    ├── For each candidate model:
   175→    │     │
   176→    │     ├── ProviderHealthRegistry::is_available(model.provider)?
   177→    │     │     YES → include in candidate set
   178→    │     │     NO  → exclude (circuit is Open)
   179→    │     │
   180→    │     └── Score candidate using stage algorithm
   181→    │
   182→    └── Select highest-scoring available candidate
   183→```
   184→
   185→If all providers for a desired model tier are unavailable, the router escalates to the next tier or returns the fallback model from the `CascadeModel`.
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→## Exponential Backoff
   190→
   191→When a circuit breaker reopens after a failed half-open probe, the cooldown period increases exponentially:
   192→
   193→```
   194→cooldown(n) = base_cooldown × 2^(n-1)
   195→```
   196→
   197→where `n` is the number of consecutive open→half-open→open cycles. This prevents the system from hammering a persistently failing provider with probe requests.
   198→
   199→| Cycle | Cooldown (RateLimit) | Cooldown (ServerError) |
   200→|-------|---------------------|----------------------|
   201→| 1 | 60s | 120s |
   202→| 2 | 120s | 240s |
   203→| 3 | 240s | 480s |
   204→| 4 | 480s (max) | 480s (max) |
   205→
   206→The maximum cooldown is capped at 480 seconds (8 minutes) to ensure eventual re-probing even for persistently failing providers.
   207→
   208→---
   209→
   210→## ProviderHealthTracker
   211→
   212→The `ProviderHealthTracker` extends the registry with time-series health metrics for dashboard visualization:
   213→
   214→```
   215→Provider: anthropic
   216→├── State: Closed
   217→├── Success rate (1h): 98.2%
   218→├── Failure rate (1h): 1.8%
   219→├── Recent errors: [Timeout × 1, RateLimit × 2]
   220→├── Avg latency (1h): 1,240ms
   221→└── Circuit opens (24h): 2
   222→```
   223→
   224→This data feeds into the learning dashboard described in [16-heartbeat](../16-heartbeat/INDEX.md) and the conductor subsystem described in [07-conductor](../07-conductor/INDEX.md).
   225→
   226→---
   227→
   228→## Anomaly Detection Integration
   229→
   230→The `AnomalyDetector` in `anomaly.rs` provides additional provider-health-adjacent checks:
   231→
   232→```rust
   233→pub struct AnomalyDetector {
   234→    prompt_hash_window: VecDeque<u64>,    // last 20 prompt hashes
   235→    cost_ewma: EwmaState,                 // EWMA cost baseline
   236→    quality_history: VecDeque<f64>,        // rolling quality scores
   237→    session_cost_usd: f64,
   238→    session_start_ms: i64,
   239→}
   240→```
   241→
   242→Three anomaly types:
   243→
   244→| Anomaly | Detection | Threshold |
   245→|---------|-----------|-----------|
   246→| **Prompt loop** | Same prompt hash appears 5+ times in last 20 | `PROMPT_LOOP_THRESHOLD = 5` |
   247→| **Cost spike** | Z-score against EWMA baseline > 3.0 | `COST_SPIKE_Z_THRESHOLD = 3.0` |
   248→| **Quality degradation** | Recent 5 scores average < 0.5 AND drop > 0.15 vs prior 10 | Composite check |
   249→
   250→### EWMA Cost Baseline
   251→
   252→The cost spike detector uses an Exponential Weighted Moving Average with α = 0.2:
   253→
   254→```
   255→ewma_new = α × observation + (1 − α) × ewma_old
   256→z_score = (observation − ewma) / ewma_stddev
   257→```
   258→
   259→The observation is compared against the EWMA *before* the state is updated, keeping sudden spikes visible instead of folding them into the baseline immediately.
   260→

1→# Mood-Congruent Memory
     2→
     3→> How emotional state biases knowledge retrieval: the four-factor scoring model, emotional tags on Engrams, PAD cosine similarity, and the dream-memory-emotion triangle.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Built
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Daimon](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [01-pad-vector.md](./01-pad-vector.md), [07-15-percent-contrarian-retrieval.md](./07-15-percent-contrarian-retrieval.md)
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/03-daimon/02-emotion-memory.md`, `bardo-backup/prd/03-daimon/06-dream-daimon.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→Emotional state biases what agents remember. This is not a bug — it's an adaptive mechanism grounded in Bower's (1981) associative network theory and validated by Emotional RAG (2024, arXiv:2410.23041). An anxious agent should retrieve memories of past dangers; a confident agent should retrieve memories of past successes. The four-factor retrieval model integrates emotional congruence as a first-class retrieval signal alongside recency, importance, and semantic relevance.
    17→
    18→This document specifies how emotional tags are attached to Engrams (knowledge entries), how the four-factor scoring model computes retrieval priority, how PAD cosine similarity captures emotional direction, and how the dream system interacts with emotional memory through consolidation bias and depotentiation.
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Emotional Tags on Engrams
    23→
    24→### The EmotionalTag Struct
    25→
    26→Every Engram in the Neuro knowledge store gains an optional emotional tag. This tag captures the PAD vector and Plutchik classification at the time the Engram was created:
    27→
    28→```rust
    29→/// Emotional context attached to a knowledge entry at creation time.
    30→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    31→pub struct EmotionalTag {
    32→    /// PAD vector at the time the Engram was created.
    33→    pub pad: PadVector,
    34→
    35→    /// Plutchik label for human-readable annotation.
    36→    /// e.g., "moderate_fear", "mild_joy", "strong_surprise"
    37→    pub emotion: String,
    38→
    39→    /// Emotional intensity at encoding time [0.0, 1.0].
    40→    /// Higher intensity means the Engram was encoded under
    41→    /// stronger emotional conditions and receives higher
    42→    /// consolidation priority.
    43→    pub intensity: f32,
    44→
    45→    /// Brief description of the appraisal trigger.
    46→    /// e.g., "gate_fail:rung_2:task_abc"
    47→    pub trigger: String,
    48→
    49→    /// Mood snapshot at the time of creation.
    50→    /// Captures the broader affective context beyond the
    51→    /// discrete emotion. Used for mood-congruent retrieval:
    52→    /// the mood at encoding is compared with the mood at
    53→    /// retrieval to compute congruence.
    54→    pub mood_snapshot: PadVector,
    55→}
    56→```
    57→
    58→The tag is optional — Engrams created before the Daimon is enabled, or during T0 ticks that skip appraisal, have `emotional_tag: None`. The emotional component defaults to a neutral factor (0.5) in the retrieval score for untagged entries.
    59→
    60→### Extension to Engram Storage
    61→
    62→The Engram storage schema (whether in SQLite, JSONL, or the Substrate) includes affect provenance columns:
    63→
    64→```
    65→affect_pleasure     REAL    -- PAD pleasure at discovery
    66→affect_arousal      REAL    -- PAD arousal at discovery
    67→affect_dominance    REAL    -- PAD dominance at discovery
    68→discovery_emotion   TEXT    -- Plutchik label at time of creation
    69→```
    70→
    71→These columns enable the four-factor retrieval scoring. They are indexed for efficient filtering (e.g., "all Engrams where arousal > 0.5").
    72→
    73→---
    74→
    75→## Four-Factor Retrieval Scoring
    76→
    77→Every knowledge retrieval scores candidates using four factors, each grounded in a different research tradition:
    78→
    79→### Factor 1: Recency (Ebbinghaus 1885)
    80→
    81→Memories fade with time. The forgetting curve is exponential:
    82→
    83→```
    84→recency = exp(-t / half_life)
    85→```
    86→
    87→where `t` is time since last access. Recently accessed entries score higher. Half-life is type-dependent (Warnings: 7 days; Insights: 30 days; Facts: 365 days) and tier-multiplied (Transient: 0.1×; Working: 0.5×; Consolidated: 1.0×; Persistent: 5.0×).
    88→
    89→### Factor 2: Importance (Shinn et al. 2023)
    90→
    91→Entries validated through operational use earn higher confidence. The quality score combines raw confidence with the validation ratio:
    92→
    93→```
    94→quality = confidence × (validated / (validated + contradicted + 1))
    95→```
    96→
    97→This implements Reflexion's (Shinn et al. 2023) core insight: self-reflection on past performance improves future decisions. Knowledge that predicted correct outcomes is more valuable than knowledge that has never been tested.
    98→
    99→### Factor 3: Relevance (Standard RAG)
   100→
   101→Cosine similarity between the query embedding and the entry embedding. This is the baseline retrieval signal — how semantically close is this entry to the agent's current task?
   102→
   103→### Factor 4: Emotional Congruence (Bower 1981)
   104→
   105→PAD cosine similarity between the agent's current emotional state and the entry's affect provenance:
   106→
   107→```rust
   108→/// Four-factor retrieval scoring.
   109→///
   110→/// score = w_recency    × recency(Ebbinghaus)
   111→///       + w_importance × quality(Reflexion)
   112→///       + w_relevance  × cosine(query, entry)
   113→///       + w_emotional  × PAD_cosine(current_mood, entry_affect)
   114→///
   115→/// Initial weights: recency: 0.20, importance: 0.25, relevance: 0.35, emotional: 0.20
   116→/// Weights are learned by the self-tuning system over time.
   117→pub fn score_entry(
   118→    entry: &KnowledgeEntry,
   119→    query_embedding: &[f32],
   120→    current_pad: &PadVector,
   121→    current_tick: u64,
   122→    weights: &RetrievalWeights,
   123→) -> f64 {
   124→    let recency = (-((current_tick - entry.last_accessed_at) as f64)
   125→        / weights.recency_half_life).exp();
   126→
   127→    let importance = entry.quality_score();
   128→
   129→    let relevance = cosine_similarity(query_embedding, &entry.embedding);
   130→
   131→    let emotional_congruence = pad_cosine_similarity(current_pad, &entry.affect_pad());
   132→
   133→    weights.recency * recency
   134→        + weights.importance * importance
   135→        + weights.relevance * relevance
   136→        + weights.emotional * emotional_congruence
   137→}
   138→```
   139→
   140→### Weight Distribution
   141→
   142→The initial weights (0.20, 0.25, 0.35, 0.20) assign 35% to semantic relevance, 25% to importance, 20% to recency, and 20% to emotional congruence. These are learned over time — the self-tuning system adjusts weights based on which factor combinations correlate with positive task outcomes.
   143→
   144→The 20% emotional weight is significant but not dominant. It's large enough to bias retrieval toward mood-congruent entries (5–30% accuracy boost per Bower 1981) without overwhelming semantic relevance. An emotionally congruent but semantically irrelevant entry won't surface — the 35% relevance weight ensures topical appropriateness.
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## PAD Cosine Similarity
   149→
   150→The PAD cosine similarity function captures the *direction* of emotional state rather than its *magnitude*. Two anxiety states at different intensities are recognized as similar. Two states with the same intensity but different directions (anxiety vs. confidence) are recognized as different.
   151→
   152→```rust
   153→/// PAD cosine similarity, mapped to [0, 1].
   154→///
   155→/// Captures the direction of emotional state (quality of emotion)
   156→/// rather than its magnitude (intensity). An anxiety state at
   157→/// intensity 0.3 is similar to anxiety at intensity 0.8.
   158→pub fn pad_cosine_similarity(a: &PadVector, b: &PadVector) -> f64 {
   159→    let dot = a.pleasure * b.pleasure
   160→        + a.arousal * b.arousal
   161→        + a.dominance * b.dominance;
   162→
   163→    let mag_a = (a.pleasure.powi(2) + a.arousal.powi(2) + a.dominance.powi(2)).sqrt();
   164→    let mag_b = (b.pleasure.powi(2) + b.arousal.powi(2) + b.dominance.powi(2)).sqrt();
   165→
   166→    if mag_a == 0.0 || mag_b == 0.0 {
   167→        return 0.5;  // Neutral mood → neutral similarity
   168→    }
   169→
   170→    // Map cosine similarity from [-1, 1] to [0, 1]
   171→    (dot / (mag_a * mag_b) + 1.0) / 2.0
   172→}
   173→```
   174→
   175→**Why cosine, not Euclidean?**: Euclidean distance in PAD space conflates emotional direction with emotional intensity. An agent at P: -0.2, A: +0.1, D: -0.1 (mild anxiety) and an agent at P: -0.8, A: +0.5, D: -0.4 (strong anxiety) have high Euclidean distance (0.74) but high cosine similarity (0.99). They're both anxious — the memories encoded under either state are relevant to the other. Cosine similarity captures this relationship.
   176→
   177→---
   178→
   179→## The Full Retrieval Pipeline
   180→
   181→The complete retrieval pipeline integrates four-factor scoring with contrarian injection:
   182→
   183→```
   184→Phase 1: CANDIDATE GENERATION
   185→    HNSW approximate nearest neighbors (3× overfetch)
   186→    → 3 × limit candidates
   187→
   188→Phase 2: FOUR-FACTOR RE-RANKING
   189→    Score each candidate with recency × importance × relevance × emotional congruence
   190→    → Sorted by composite score
   191→
   192→Phase 3: CONTRARIAN INJECTION (if tracker says inject)
   193→    Invert pleasure and dominance of current PAD
   194→    Search for opposite-valence entries
   195→    Add to candidate set and re-rank
   196→    → Ensures 15% minimum opposite-valence in rolling 200-tick window
   197→
   198→Phase 4: RETRIEVAL STRENGTHENING (Testing Effect)
   199→    Mark retrieved entries as accessed
   200→    Increment access count
   201→    → Roediger & Karpicke (2006): retrieval strengthens memory trace
   202→
   203→Phase 5: RETURN top-k results
   204→```
   205→
   206→### Cross-Emotional Retrieval
   207→
   208→The system supports explicit cross-emotional retrieval — deliberately searching for memories encoded under a different emotional state. This is available as a named retrieval mode for specific situations:
   209→
   210→| Situation | Cross-Emotional Query | Rationale |
   211→|---|---|---|
   212→| Anxious agent, approaching deadline | Retrieve confident memories | Coping: recall successful strategies under pressure |
   213→| Overconfident agent, novel territory | Retrieve cautious memories | Humility: recall past overconfidence failures |
   214→| Stuck agent, no progress | Retrieve diverse emotional contexts | Divergence: inject variety to break patterns |
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## Emotional Consolidation Bias
   219→
   220→When the Daimon is enabled, the dream consolidation process applies an emotional salience boost. High-arousal experiences are preferentially consolidated, matching the neurobiological pattern where emotional arousal enhances memory consolidation via amygdala-hippocampal interaction (McGaugh 2004):
   221→
   222→```rust
   223→pub fn consolidation_priority(episode: &Episode) -> f64 {
   224→    let base_priority = episode.importance * episode.novelty;
   225→
   226→    let emotional_tag = match &episode.emotional_tag {
   227→        Some(tag) => tag,
   228→        None => return base_priority,
   229→    };
   230→
   231→    // Emotional arousal increases consolidation priority.
   232→    let arousal_boost = emotional_tag.pad.arousal.abs() as f64 * 0.3;
   233→    base_priority * (1.0 + arousal_boost)
   234→}
   235→```
   236→
   237→The 0.3 scaling factor ensures emotional salience is *additive* to the existing importance-novelty ranking, not dominant. Among episodes of comparable importance and novelty, emotional intensity breaks the tie.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## Emotional Provenance Tracking
   242→
   243→When episodes are consolidated into Insights and Heuristics, the emotional provenance transfers:
   244→
   245→```rust
   246→pub struct EmotionalProvenance {
   247→    /// Average PAD vector during evidence accumulation.
   248→    pub average_pad: PadVector,
   249→
   250→    /// Emotion at first discovery.
   251→    pub discovery_emotion: String,
   252→
   253→    /// Narrative arc of validation.
   254→    pub validation_arc: Option<ValidationArc>,
   255→
   256→    /// Emotional diversity: Shannon entropy across supporting episodes.
   257→    /// Higher = more reliable (validated across diverse emotional states).
   258→    pub emotional_diversity: f64,
   259→}
   260→
   261→pub enum ValidationArc {
   262→    /// Adversity to positive outcome — most transferable knowledge.
   263→    Redemptive,
   264→    /// Initial success to failure — cautionary.
   265→    Contaminating,
   266→    /// Consistent tone — reliable but less narratively rich.
   267→    Stable,
   268→    /// Gradual improvement — successful learning trajectory.
   269→    Progressive,
   270→}
   271→```
   272→
   273→### Emotional Diversity as Quality Signal
   274→
   275→An Insight validated across diverse emotional states is more reliable than one validated only during a single mood. Emotional diversity is computed as normalized Shannon entropy of emotional labels across supporting episodes:
   276→
   277→```rust
   278→pub fn emotional_diversity(supporting_episodes: &[Episode]) -> f64 {
   279→    let mut emotion_counts: HashMap<String, u32> = HashMap::new();
   280→    for ep in supporting_episodes {
   281→        if let Some(ref tag) = ep.emotional_tag {
   282→            *emotion_counts.entry(tag.emotion.clone()).or_insert(0) += 1;
   283→        }
   284→    }
   285→
   286→    let total: u32 = emotion_counts.values().sum();
   287→    if total == 0 { return 0.0; }
   288→
   289→    let mut entropy = 0.0_f64;
   290→    for &count in emotion_counts.values() {
   291→        let p = count as f64 / total as f64;
   292→        if p > 0.0 {
   293→            entropy -= p * p.log2();
   294→        }
   295→    }
   296→
   297→    let max_entropy = (emotion_counts.len() as f64).log2();
   298→    if max_entropy > 0.0 { entropy / max_entropy } else { 0.0 }
   299→}
   300→```
   301→
   302→A diversity score of 1.0 means every supporting episode had a different emotional label — maximum diversity, highest reliability signal. A score of 0.0 means all episodes had the same label — potential emotional bias in the validation.
   303→
   304→---
   305→
   306→## The Dream-Memory-Emotion Triangle
   307→
   308→Dreams, memory, and emotion form a three-way interaction:
   309→
   310→1. **Emotion → Memory**: Emotional state biases which memories are retrieved (mood-congruent retrieval) and which are consolidated (arousal-based consolidation priority).
   311→
   312→2. **Memory → Emotion**: Retrieved memories influence the agent's current emotional state. Retrieving past failures increases anxiety; retrieving past successes increases confidence. This is the appraisal pipeline — events are appraised, and retrieved context shapes how events are interpreted.
   313→
   314→3. **Dreams → Memory + Emotion**: Dream consolidation reorganizes memory (promoting, pruning, synthesizing) and reshapes emotion (REM depotentiation reduces arousal on charged memories). Dreams also create new somatic markers from emotionally significant episodes.
   315→
   316→This triangle is self-regulating when all three mechanisms operate:
   317→- Mood-congruent retrieval provides adaptive bias (useful in most cases)
   318→- Contrarian retrieval prevents the bias from becoming a trap
   319→- Dream depotentiation cools the fuel that drives the bias
   320→- Consolidation strengthens the most emotionally significant memories
   321→
   322→Without dreams, the triangle degenerates: emotional memories accumulate without processing, mood-congruent retrieval creates increasingly strong feedback loops, and the contrarian mechanism becomes the only defense against rumination.
   323→
   324→---
   325→

   329→- Blaney, P.H. (1986). "Affect and Memory: A Review." *Psychological Bulletin*, 99(2), 229–246.
   330→- Faul, L. & LaBar, K.S. (2022). "Mood-Congruent Memory Revisited." *Psychological Review*.
   331→- Emotional RAG. (2024). "Emotional RAG: Enhancing Role-Playing Agents through Emotional Retrieval." arXiv:2410.23041.
   332→- Ebbinghaus, H. (1885). *Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology*.
   333→- Shinn, N. et al. (2023). "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning." *NeurIPS*.
   334→- McGaugh, J.L. (2004). "The Amygdala Modulates the Consolidation of Memories of Emotionally Arousing Experiences." *Annual Review of Neuroscience*, 27.
   335→- Roediger, H.L. & Karpicke, J.D. (2006). "Test-enhanced learning: Taking memory tests improves long-term retention." *Psychological Science*, 17(3), 249–255.
   336→- McAdams, D.P. (2001). "The Psychology of Life Stories." *Review of General Psychology*, 5(2).
   337→- Abelson, R.P. (1963). "Computer Simulation of 'Hot Cognition'." In Tomkins & Messick (Eds.), *Computer Simulation of Personality*. Wiley.

1→# The Substrate Trait
     2→
     3→> **Abstract:** The Substrate trait is the persistence layer of the Synapse Architecture —
     4→> every Engram in Roko is stored in and retrieved from a Substrate. This document provides
     5→> the full trait specification, describes each method with its semantics, lists all current
     6→> and planned implementations, explains the query model, and covers pruning, idempotence,
     7→> and concurrency guarantees.
     8→
     9→
    10→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## 1. Role in the Architecture
    15→
    16→Substrate is the foundational trait — it provides the "ground" that all other traits operate
    17→over. Without Substrate, there is nowhere to store Engrams, nowhere to query for candidates,
    18→and no way to persist results. It maps to **System 1 (Operations)** in Beer's Viable System
    19→Model (Beer 1972) and to the **working memory** and **episodic memory** components of CoALA
    20→(Sumers et al. 2023, arXiv:2309.02427).
    21→
    22→Substrate is the only async trait that appears in step 1 (PERCEIVE) and step 7 (PERSIST) of
    23→the universal cognitive loop — it bookends the entire cycle.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## 2. Full Trait Specification
    28→
    29→From `roko-core/src/traits.rs`:
    30→
    31→```rust
    32→#[async_trait]
    33→pub trait Substrate: Send + Sync {
    34→    /// Store an Engram. Returns its content hash. Idempotent on content.
    35→    async fn put(&self, signal: Signal) -> Result<ContentHash>;
    36→
    37→    /// Retrieve an Engram by content hash. Does not apply decay.
    38→    async fn get(&self, id: &ContentHash) -> Result<Option<Signal>>;
    39→
    40→    /// Query for Engrams matching the given filter. Impls may apply decay
    41→    /// when evaluating min_weight and when ordering results.
    42→    async fn query(&self, q: &Query, ctx: &Context) -> Result<Vec<Signal>>;
    43→
    44→    /// Remove Engrams whose effective weight (score × decay) has fallen
    45→    /// below threshold at ctx.now_ms. Returns count of pruned Engrams.
    46→    async fn prune(&self, threshold: f32, ctx: &Context) -> Result<usize>;
    47→
    48→    /// Optional: total count of stored Engrams.
    49→    async fn len(&self) -> Result<usize> { Ok(0) }
    50→
    51→    /// Optional: is the substrate empty?
    52→    async fn is_empty(&self) -> Result<bool> { Ok(self.len().await? == 0) }
    53→
    54→    /// Human-readable name for logging/debugging.
    55→    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "unnamed_substrate" }
    56→}
    57→```
    58→
    59→### 2.1 put() — Store
    60→
    61→Stores an Engram and returns its ContentHash. Idempotent — storing the same Engram twice
    62→(same ContentHash) is a no-op. This follows from content-addressed storage: identity IS
    63→content.
    64→
    65→### 2.2 get() — Retrieve
    66→
    67→Retrieves a single Engram by its ContentHash. Returns `None` if the Engram is not found
    68→(either never stored or pruned). Does NOT apply decay — the raw Engram is returned as stored.
    69→Decay is applied at query time, not at retrieval time.
    70→
    71→### 2.3 query() — Filter and Retrieve
    72→
    73→The primary read path. The `Query` struct provides filters:
    74→
    75→```rust
    76→pub struct Query {
    77→    pub kinds: Option<Vec<Kind>>,     // filter by Engram kind
    78→    pub author: Option<String>,        // filter by author
    79→    pub session: Option<String>,       // filter by session
    80→    pub since_ms: Option<i64>,         // created after this timestamp
    81→    pub until_ms: Option<i64>,         // created before this timestamp
    82→    pub min_weight: Option<f32>,       // minimum effective weight (score × decay)
    83→    pub tags: Vec<(String, String)>,   // all tags must match
    84→    pub limit: Option<usize>,          // maximum results
    85→}
    86→```
    87→
    88→All filters AND together. An empty `Query::all()` matches everything. Implementations may
    89→apply decay when evaluating `min_weight` and when ordering results.
    90→
    91→### 2.4 prune() — Garbage Collection
    92→
    93→Removes Engrams whose effective weight has fallen below the threshold:
    94→
    95→```
    96→weight = score.effective() × decay.apply(ctx.now_ms - created_at_ms)
    97→```
    98→
    99→Returns the count of pruned Engrams. This is automatic memory management — the system
   100→forgets information that has decayed below the threshold of relevance.
   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→## 3. Implementations
   105→
   106→### 3.1 MemorySubstrate
   107→
   108→In-memory HashMap backend for testing. Fast, ephemeral, single-process.
   109→
   110→```rust
   111→pub struct MemorySubstrate {
   112→    signals: RwLock<HashMap<ContentHash, Signal>>,
   113→}
   114→```
   115→
   116→### 3.2 FileSubstrate (roko-fs)
   117→
   118→JSONL file backend for default persistence. Each Engram is one line in `.roko/signals.jsonl`.
   119→Provides append-only writes for crash safety and periodic compaction via `prune()`.
   120→
   121→Located in `roko-fs`. This is the default Substrate for all Roko agents.
   122→
   123→### 3.3 HdcSubstrate (Planned)
   124→
   125→Hyperdimensional Computing substrate for semantic similarity queries. Engrams are encoded
   126→as 10,240-bit HDC vectors using XOR bind and majority bundle (Kanerva 2009, Cognitive
   127→Computation 1(2)). Queries use Hamming distance for O(1) similarity comparison.
   128→
   129→### 3.4 ChainSubstrate (Planned)
   130→
   131→On-chain Substrate on the Korai chain. Engram ContentHashes are posted on-chain for
   132→attestation and shared state. Full Engram bodies are stored off-chain (IPFS or similar)
   133→with on-chain pointers.
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## 4. Concurrency
   138→
   139→All Substrates are `Send + Sync`. Implementations must handle concurrent access internally:
   140→
   141→- `MemorySubstrate` uses `RwLock<HashMap<...>>`
   142→- `FileSubstrate` uses append-only writes with periodic compaction
   143→- Future network Substrates use message passing or distributed locks
   144→
   145→The three cognitive speeds (Gamma/Theta/Delta) run on separate async tasks and may access
   146→the same Substrate concurrently. The `Send + Sync` bounds ensure this is safe.
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→## Academic Foundations
   151→
   152→| Citation | Contribution |
   153→|---|---|
   154→| Beer 1972, Brain of the Firm | VSM System 1 (Operations): the operational substrate. |
   155→| Sumers et al. 2023 (arXiv:2309.02427) | CoALA: working memory and episodic memory components. |
   156→| Kanerva 2009, Cognitive Computation 1(2) | HDC: hyperdimensional computing for similarity search. |
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## Current Status and Gaps
   161→
   162→- **Implemented**: `MemorySubstrate` in `roko-std`, `FileSubstrate` in `roko-fs` (37 tests).
   163→- **Not implemented**: `HdcSubstrate`, `ChainSubstrate`.
   164→
   165→---
