1→# Pareto Frontier Pruning
     2→
     3→> **Crate:** `roko-learn` · **Module:** `pareto.rs`
     4→> **Wiring:** `CascadeRouter` calls `compute_pareto_frontier()` every 50 observations
     5→> **Implementation plan:** `modelrouting/08-learning-loops.md` (task 2G.11)
     6→> **Cross-references:** [03-bandits-ucb-thompson-linucb](03-bandits-ucb-thompson-linucb.md), [04-cascade-router](04-cascade-router.md), [08-cost-normalization](08-cost-normalization.md)
     7→
     8→
     9→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Purpose
    14→
    15→Pareto frontier pruning identifies which models are non-dominated with respect to two objectives: pass rate and cost per successful task. A model is Pareto-optimal if no other model has both a higher pass rate and a lower cost per successful task. Dominated models (worse on both metrics than some other model) are pruned from the candidate set before presenting arms to the bandit.
    16→
    17→This serves two functions:
    18→1. **Reduces exploration waste** — the bandit doesn't spend trials on clearly inferior models.
    19→2. **Focuses the tradeoff** — the remaining Pareto-optimal models represent genuine cost-quality tradeoffs that the bandit must resolve.
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Dominance Definition
    24→
    25→Model A dominates model B when:
    26→- A has pass_rate ≥ B's pass_rate, AND
    27→- A has cost_per_success ≤ B's cost_per_success, AND
    28→- At least one inequality is strict.
    29→
    30→```
    31→Model A: pass_rate=0.90, cost/success=$10.00
    32→Model B: pass_rate=0.70, cost/success=$12.00
    33→Model C: pass_rate=0.80, cost/success=$9.00
    34→
    35→A dominates B (higher pass rate AND lower cost).
    36→Neither A nor C dominates the other:
    37→  A has higher pass rate, but C has lower cost.
    38→  → Both are Pareto-optimal.
    39→```
    40→
    41→---
    42→
    43→## Algorithm
    44→
    45→```rust
    46→pub fn compute_pareto_frontier(
    47→    stats: &HashMap<String, ModelObservation>
    48→) -> Vec<String> {
    49→    let mut frontier = Vec::new();
    50→
    51→    for (slug_a, obs_a) in stats {
    52→        let dominated = stats.iter().any(|(slug_b, obs_b)| {
    53→            slug_b != slug_a
    54→                && obs_b.pass_rate >= obs_a.pass_rate
    55→                && obs_b.cost_per_success <= obs_a.cost_per_success
    56→                && (obs_b.pass_rate > obs_a.pass_rate
    57→                    || obs_b.cost_per_success < obs_a.cost_per_success)
    58→        });
    59→
    60→        if !dominated {
    61→            frontier.push(slug_a.clone());
    62→        }
    63→    }
    64→
    65→    frontier.sort();
    66→    frontier
    67→}
    68→```
    69→
    70→The algorithm is O(n²) where n is the number of models. With typical model counts (3-10), this is negligible.
    71→
    72→### ModelObservation
    73→
    74→```rust
    75→pub struct ModelObservation {
    76→    /// Fraction of tasks that passed.
    77→    pub pass_rate: f64,
    78→    /// Total cost divided by number of successful tasks.
    79→    pub cost_per_success: f64,
    80→    /// Average latency in milliseconds.
    81→    pub avg_latency_ms: f64,
    82→    /// Number of observations contributing to this summary.
    83→    pub observations: u64,
    84→}
    85→```
    86→
    87→Note that `avg_latency_ms` is tracked but not currently used in the dominance check. Future extensions may include latency as a third Pareto dimension, creating a three-objective frontier.
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Visualization
    92→
    93→```
    94→Pass Rate ↑
    95→    1.0 │         ● A (Pareto-optimal)
    96→        │
    97→    0.8 │    ● C (Pareto-optimal)
    98→        │
    99→    0.7 │              ✗ B (dominated by A)
   100→        │
   101→    0.6 │
   102→        │
   103→    0.0 └────────────────────────────────► Cost/Success
   104→        $0   $5    $9   $10   $12   $15
   105→```
   106→
   107→The Pareto frontier is the upper-left boundary of the point cloud. Points below and to the right of any frontier point are dominated.
   108→
   109→---
   110→
   111→## Integration with Cascade Router
   112→
   113→The cascade router recomputes the Pareto frontier every `PARETO_RECOMPUTE_INTERVAL = 50` observations:
   114→
   115→```
   116→CascadeRouter::update(model, reward, cost)
   117→    │
   118→    ├── Update model stats (trials, successes, costs)
   119→    │
   120→    ├── if observations % 50 == 0:
   121→    │       │
   122→    │       ├── Collect ModelObservation for each model
   123→    │       │     pass_rate = successes / trials
   124→    │       │     cost_per_success = total_cost / successes
   125→    │       │
   126→    │       └── pareto_frontier = compute_pareto_frontier(observations)
   127→    │
   128→    └── Store frontier for use in next select() call
   129→```
   130→
   131→During `select()`, only models on the Pareto frontier are presented as candidates to the stage-2 or stage-3 routing algorithm. Models that fell off the frontier are excluded from consideration until the next recomputation (which may restore them if other models' statistics change).
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Multi-Objective Extension
   136→
   137→The current implementation uses a two-objective Pareto frontier (pass_rate, cost_per_success). The implementation plan (modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md, task 2J.13) describes a multi-objective Pareto bandit that extends this to four dimensions:
   138→
   139→| Objective | Direction | Weight |
   140→|-----------|-----------|--------|
   141→| Quality (pass rate) | Maximize | Configurable |
   142→| Cost per success | Minimize | Configurable |
   143→| Latency (p50) | Minimize | Configurable |
   144→| Reliability (1 − error rate) | Maximize | Configurable |
   145→
   146→The multi-objective extension uses scalarization: each objective is weighted and combined into a single score, and the Pareto frontier is computed over the scalarized scores. This preserves the O(n²) complexity while enabling richer tradeoff analysis.
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→## Edge Cases
   151→
   152→### All Models Dominated
   153→
   154→If the model set contains a single dominant model (highest pass rate AND lowest cost), all other models are dominated and the frontier contains only one model. In this case, the bandit has no choice to make — the dominant model is always selected. This is the expected steady-state for mature systems where one model clearly outperforms alternatives.
   155→
   156→### Insufficient Observations
   157→
   158→Models with very few observations have noisy statistics. A model that happened to succeed on its first 3 trials appears to have a 100% pass rate, potentially dominating models with hundreds of observations and a 90% pass rate. The cascade router mitigates this by requiring a minimum observation count before including a model in the Pareto computation. Models below this threshold are always included in the candidate set (exploration) regardless of dominance.
   159→
   160→### New Models
   161→
   162→When a new model is added to the system (e.g., a provider releases a new model version), it starts with zero observations and is excluded from Pareto computation. The bandit gives it maximum exploration priority (UCB1 selects unpulled arms first), ensuring it accumulates enough data for Pareto evaluation within the first 50 observations.
   163→
   164→---
   165→
   166→## Frontier Evolution Over Time
   167→
   168→The Pareto frontier is not static — it evolves as the system accumulates observations and as models change.
   169→
   170→### Cold Start
   171→
   172→At system start with no observations, all models are on the Pareto frontier (no model has enough data to be dominated). The bandit explores uniformly.
   173→
   174→### Convergence Phase (50-200 observations)
   175→
   176→As statistics accumulate, dominated models begin to fall off the frontier. Typically, the model set converges to 2-3 Pareto-optimal models representing genuine tradeoffs (e.g., cheap-but-lower-quality vs. expensive-but-higher-quality).
   177→
   178→### Steady State (200+ observations)
   179→
   180→The frontier stabilizes. Changes occur when:
   181→- A provider updates a model (changing its quality or cost characteristics).
   182→- A new model is added to the system.
   183→- The task mix changes (altering the observed pass rates).
   184→
   185→### Provider Updates
   186→
   187→When a provider deploys a new model version, the model's historical statistics may no longer reflect its current performance. The cascade router handles this by:
   188→1. Detecting the model version change (via model slug comparison).
   189→2. Discounting old observations (partial reset of the model's stats).
   190→3. Re-including the model in the Pareto computation with reduced weight.
   191→
   192→This ensures that a model that was previously dominated but has been improved by its provider gets a fair chance to re-enter the frontier.
   193→
   194→---
   195→
   196→## Practical Example
   197→
   198→Consider a system with four models after 300 observations:
   199→
   200→```
   201→Model               Pass Rate   Cost/Success   On Frontier?
   202→─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   203→claude-haiku-4.5     0.78        $0.12          YES (cheapest)
   204→claude-sonnet-4      0.86        $0.95          YES (mid-range)
   205→claude-opus-4        0.91        $2.40          YES (highest quality)
   206→deepseek-chat        0.72        $0.45          NO (dominated by haiku)
   207→```
   208→
   209→Deepseek is dominated by haiku (haiku has both higher pass rate AND lower cost), so it's pruned from the candidate set. The bandit only considers haiku, sonnet, and opus — three models representing the genuine cost-quality tradeoff.
   210→
   211→After a provider update where deepseek improves to 0.85 pass rate:
   212→
   213→```
   214→Model               Pass Rate   Cost/Success   On Frontier?
   215→─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   216→claude-haiku-4.5     0.78        $0.12          YES (cheapest)
   217→deepseek-chat        0.85        $0.45          YES (new: better than haiku, cheaper than sonnet)
   218→claude-sonnet-4      0.86        $0.95          NO (dominated by deepseek!)
   219→claude-opus-4        0.91        $2.40          YES (highest quality)
   220→```
   221→
   222→Now sonnet is dominated by deepseek (deepseek has nearly the same pass rate at half the cost), and deepseek enters the frontier. The bandit shifts exploration toward deepseek.
   223→
   224→---

1→# Integration Points
     2→
     3→> The four ways the Daimon's PAD vector drives other systems: behavioral state selection, tier routing bias, VCG auction bidding, and somatic landscape querying.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Built
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Daimon](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [04-six-behavioral-states.md](./04-six-behavioral-states.md), [05-behavioral-state-to-tier-routing.md](./05-behavioral-state-to-tier-routing.md), [06-somatic-markers-damasio.md](./06-somatic-markers-damasio.md)
    10→**Key sources**: `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §2, `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md` §II–III, `roko-daimon/src/lib.rs`, `roko-golem/src/daimon.rs`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→The Daimon's PAD vector is not a display value. It is a control signal that drives four systems simultaneously. Each system reads a different projection of the same PAD state:
    17→
    18→1. **Behavioral state selection** — maps the PAD vector to one of six discrete states, used for self-model reporting and TUI display
    19→2. **Tier routing bias** — modulates the CascadeRouter's prediction error thresholds, controlling compute allocation
    20→3. **VCG auction bidding** — biases context window allocation through urgency and affect weight multipliers
    21→4. **Somatic landscape querying** — provides fast heuristic pre-evaluation before analytical reasoning
    22→
    23→These are not independent features — they are different consumers of the same signal. Changing the PAD vector changes all four simultaneously. This is the Daimon's architectural contribution: a single emotional state creates coherent behavioral change across the entire cognitive pipeline.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Integration Point 1: Behavioral State Selection
    28→
    29→### Mechanism
    30→
    31→The PAD vector maps to one of six behavioral states (Engaged, Struggling, Coasting, Exploring, Focused, Resting) through threshold-based classification:
    32→
    33→```
    34→PAD → classify_behavioral_state() → BehavioralState
    35→```
    36→
    37→### What It Drives
    38→
    39→- **Self-model**: The agent's internal representation of its own cognitive state. Used in the `<daimon>` context block injected into LLM prompts via the SystemPromptBuilder.
    40→- **TUI display**: The behavioral state label appears in the dashboard and is reflected in the Spectre creature's visual state.
    41→- **Conversational tone**: Each state maps to a PAD octant, which maps to a conversational style (see `07-runtime-daimon.md`). An Anxious agent hedges extensively; a Confident agent uses definitive language.
    42→
    43→### Current Implementation
    44→
    45→In `roko-daimon/src/lib.rs`, the `modulate()` method implements behavioral state selection implicitly through PAD threshold checks:
    46→
    47→```rust
    48→fn modulate(&self, params: &mut DispatchParams) {
    49→    let state = self.query();
    50→
    51→    if state.confidence < 0.30 || state.pad.dominance < -0.25 {
    52→        // Struggling → Escalating
    53→        params.strategy = DispatchStrategy::Escalating;
    54→        params.turn_limit = params.turn_limit.saturating_add(10);
    55→        params.model = promote_model(&params.model);
    56→    } else if state.pad.pleasure > 0.35 && state.confidence > 0.65 {
    57→        // Coasting → Exploratory
    58→        params.strategy = DispatchStrategy::Exploratory;
    59→        params.turn_limit = params.turn_limit.saturating_sub(5);
    60→        params.model = demote_model(&params.model);
    61→    } else if state.pad.pleasure < -0.30 && state.pad.arousal > 0.30 {
    62→        // Struggling → Conservative
    63→        params.strategy = DispatchStrategy::Conservative;
    64→        params.turn_limit = params.turn_limit.saturating_sub(3);
    65→        params.model = demote_model(&params.model);
    66→    } else if state.pad.arousal < -0.20 {
    67→        // Resting → Proactive
    68→        params.strategy = DispatchStrategy::Proactive;
    69→        params.turn_limit = params.turn_limit.saturating_add(5);
    70→    } else {
    71→        // Engaged → Balanced
    72→        params.strategy = DispatchStrategy::Balanced;
    73→    }
    74→
    75→    params.effort = params.strategy.effort_label().to_string();
    76→}
    77→```
    78→
    79→In `roko-golem/src/daimon.rs`, the `AffectOctant::behavior_modulation()` method provides the parallel implementation using octant-based classification.
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## Integration Point 2: Tier Routing Bias
    84→
    85→### Mechanism
    86→
    87→The behavioral state modulates the CascadeRouter's prediction error thresholds:
    88→
    89→```
    90→PAD → behavioral_state → adjusted_thresholds(state) → CascadeRouter.select_tier()
    91→```
    92→
    93→### What It Drives
    94→
    95→- **Model selection**: Which LLM processes this operation (haiku, sonnet, opus)
    96→- **Compute cost**: T2 operations cost approximately 60× T0 operations
    97→- **Response quality**: Stronger models produce better results on complex tasks
    98→- **Latency**: T2 operations take 10–30 seconds vs. T0 at ~1ms
    99→
   100→### The VCG Connection
   101→
   102→The tier routing bias also indirectly affects VCG bidding (Integration Point 3) because the selected model tier determines how much context the operation can consume. A T2 operation with an opus-class model has a larger context window and can accommodate more VCG auction winners than a T1 operation with a haiku-class model.
   103→
   104→### Cost Impact Summary
   105→
   106→| Transition | Cost Change | When It Happens |
   107→|---|---|---|
   108→| Engaged → Struggling | ~3.5× increase | Sustained failures lower confidence below 0.30 |
   109→| Engaged → Coasting | ~0.4× decrease | Sustained successes raise pleasure above 0.35 |
   110→| Engaged → Resting | ~0.5× decrease | Low arousal (idle periods, post-dream) |
   111→| Struggling → Engaged | ~0.3× decrease | Recovery through successful outcomes |
   112→| Coasting → Engaged | ~2.5× increase | Encountering harder problems |
   113→
   114→See [05-behavioral-state-to-tier-routing.md](./05-behavioral-state-to-tier-routing.md) for full threshold tables.
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Integration Point 3: VCG Auction Bidding
   119→
   120→### Mechanism
   121→
   122→The VCG (Vickrey-Clarke-Groves) auction allocates the limited context window among competing subsystems. The Daimon biases bidding through two multipliers:
   123→
   124→```
   125→bid = expected_value × urgency × affect_weight
   126→
   127→where:
   128→  urgency = 1 + arousal × 0.5
   129→  affect_weight = 1 + 0.3 × abs(pleasure - 0.5)
   130→```
   131→
   132→### How Affect Modulates Bidding
   133→
   134→**High arousal → increased urgency**: When the agent is under pressure (high arousal from time pressure, blockers, or failures), the urgency multiplier amplifies bids for safety-related and task-critical context sections. At arousal = 0.8, urgency = 1.4 — a 40% boost. This ensures that urgent situations receive richer context.
   135→
   136→**Extreme pleasure (positive or negative) → increased affect weight**: When pleasure is far from neutral (0.5 in the [0,1] mapping), the affect weight increases. This means both very positive and very negative emotional states increase the weight of emotionally-relevant context:
   137→- High pleasure (P = 0.8): affect_weight = 1 + 0.3 × |0.8 - 0.5| = 1.09. Modest boost — success doesn't dramatically change context allocation.
   138→- Low pleasure (P = -0.3): affect_weight = 1 + 0.3 × |-0.3 - 0.5| = 1.24. Stronger boost — failure increases the weight of diagnostic context.
   139→
   140→### Per-Subsystem Bidding Behavior
   141→
   142→The Daimon's PAD state biases specific subsystems' bids:
   143→
   144→| Subsystem | High Arousal Effect | Low Dominance Effect | Low Pleasure Effect |
   145→|---|---|---|---|
   146→| **Neuro** (knowledge) | Safety knowledge prioritized | Exploratory knowledge boosted | Warning knowledge boosted |
   147→| **Daimon** (affect context) | Bid increases (own state is urgent) | Bid increases (needs self-model) | Bid increases (needs emotional context) |
   148→| **Iteration memory** | Boost for recent failures | Neutral | **Strong boost** — past failure context critical |
   149→| **Code intelligence** | Boost for safety-critical code paths | Neutral | Neutral |
   150→| **Playbook rules** | Proven playbooks prioritized | Novel playbooks boosted | Conservative playbooks prioritized |
   151→| **Research artifacts** | Neutral | **Strong boost** — research needed | Neutral |
   152→| **Task context** | PRD sections with deadlines prioritized | Neutral | Neutral |
   153→| **Oracle predictions** | Predictions with high error prioritized | Neutral | Neutral |
   154→
   155→### VCG Truthfulness Guarantee
   156→
   157→The VCG mechanism uses second-price payment: each winning bidder pays the bid of the next-highest loser. This creates truthful incentives — subsystems cannot benefit from inflating their bids because they pay based on others' bids, not their own. The Daimon's affect modulation changes the *actual value* of context (not just the bid), so it doesn't undermine truthfulness.
   158→
   159→**Citation**: Vickrey (1961), Clarke (1971), Groves (1973). Applied to attention allocation following the context engineering framework (Karpathy 2025).
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## Integration Point 4: Somatic Landscape Querying
   164→
   165→### Mechanism
   166→
   167→Before selecting an action, the agent queries the somatic landscape with the proposed strategy's 8D coordinates:
   168→
   169→```
   170→Strategy coords → SomaticLandscape.query() → SomaticSignal → pre-analytical bias
   171→```
   172→
   173→### What It Drives
   174→
   175→- **Pre-filter on strategy space**: The somatic signal narrows the action space before analytical reasoning begins
   176→- **Model tier suggestion**: Strong negative valence → suggest T2; strong positive valence → suggest T0/T1
   177→- **Review scrutiny**: Negative valence increases the gate rung level for the resulting output
   178→- **Exploration vs. exploitation**: Negative valence → prefer proven playbooks; positive valence → permit exploration
   179→
   180→### Timing in the Cognitive Pipeline
   181→
   182→The somatic query is the fastest decision signal, executing before everything else:
   183→
   184→```
   185→1. SOMATIC QUERY (< 1ms)        — k-d tree nearest neighbor
   186→2. PREDICTION ERROR PROBES (< 5ms) — 16 deterministic probes
   187→3. TIER SELECTION (~0ms)         — threshold comparison
   188→4. CONTEXT ASSEMBLY (~10ms)      — VCG auction, knowledge retrieval
   189→5. MODEL INFERENCE (~2-30s)      — LLM call at selected tier
   190→```
   191→
   192→The somatic query can preempt tier selection: if the somatic signal is strongly negative, it can force T2 before the prediction error probes even run. This is the System 1 fast path — the agent "feels" that this situation is dangerous before it has analytically assessed why.
   193→
   194→### Interaction with PAD Vector
   195→
   196→The somatic landscape and the PAD vector are complementary:
   197→
   198→| Property | PAD Vector | Somatic Landscape |
   199→|---|---|---|
   200→| Question | "How do I feel right now?" | "How did I feel last time in a situation like *this*?" |
   201→| Scope | Global mood | Situation-specific memory |
   202→| Update frequency | Every appraisal event | Dream consolidation + significant live events |
   203→| Query cost | O(1) | O(log N) |
   204→| Affects | All four integration points | Pre-analytical bias only |
   205→
   206→Both signals feed into the decision pipeline, but the somatic signal is more specific. The PAD vector says "I'm anxious." The somatic signal says "The last time I was in *this particular region of strategy space*, it went badly." The combination produces richer behavioral modulation than either signal alone.
   207→
   208→---
   209→
   210→## Integration Map
   211→
   212→```
   213→                    PAD Vector
   214→                   ╱    │    ╲
   215→                  ╱     │     ╲
   216→                 ╱      │      ╲
   217→                ╱       │       ╲
   218→               ╱        │        ╲
   219→    Behavioral      Tier         VCG        Somatic
   220→     State         Routing      Auction     Landscape
   221→       │             │            │            │
   222→       ▼             ▼            ▼            ▼
   223→   Self-model    CascadeRouter  Context     Pre-filter
   224→   TUI display   Model select   assembly    Fast bias
   225→   Tone map      Cost control   Token alloc  Strategy
   226→                                             ranking
   227→```
   228→
   229→All four paths read the same PAD state. A change to pleasure, arousal, or dominance cascades through all four integration points simultaneously, producing coherent behavioral change.
   230→
   231→---
   232→
   233→## Event Emission
   234→
   235→When the PAD state changes significantly (Euclidean delta > 0.15 from last emitted state), the system emits events:
   236→
   237→| Event | Trigger | Consumers |
   238→|---|---|---|
   239→| `MoodUpdate` | PAD Euclidean delta > 0.15 | TUI, episode logger, connected clients |
   240→| `DaimonAppraisal` | Every appraisal completes | TUI, episode logger, metrics |
   241→| `SomaticMarkerFired` | Somatic marker matches current situation | TUI (particle effect), episode logger |
   242→| `EmotionalShift` | Dominant Plutchik emotion changes | TUI, notification system |
   243→
   244→The 0.15 threshold prevents event flooding. A shift from Relaxed to Anxious (PAD distance ~1.2) always emits. Micro-fluctuations within the same octant (distance ~0.05) do not.
   245→
   246→---
   247→
   248→## Current Status and Gaps
   249→
   250→**Implemented**:
   251→- Integration Point 1 (behavioral state): Fully implemented in `roko-daimon/src/lib.rs` `modulate()` and `roko-golem/src/daimon.rs` `behavior_modulation()`.
   252→- Integration Point 2 (tier routing): Model promotion/demotion is implemented. Threshold modulation is not wired to CascadeRouter.
   253→- Signal emission: MoodUpdate events are emitted when PAD change exceeds threshold (roko-golem). Affect signals emitted to JSONL (roko-golem).
   254→
   255→**Not implemented**:
   256→- Integration Point 3 (VCG auction): The VCG auction is specified but no code exists.
   257→- Integration Point 4 (somatic landscape): The k-d tree and query protocol are specified but no code exists.
   258→- Integration Point 2 wiring: The CascadeRouter does not read Daimon thresholds.

1→# The Six Synapse Traits
     2→
     3→> **Abstract:** The Synapse Architecture is built from six composable traits — the "verbs"
     4→> that operate on Engrams. Every capability in Roko — agent spawning, gate verification,
     5→> prompt assembly, model routing, memory retrieval, pheromone reaction, chain participation —
     6→> is an implementation of one of these six traits. This document introduces the trait model,
     7→> explains how traits compose across layers, and provides the complete Rust signatures from
     8→> shipping code.
     9→
    10→
    11→> **Implementation**: Shipping
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→## 1. The Composition Model
    16→
    17→Classical agent frameworks define capabilities through inheritance hierarchies or plugin
    18→registries. A "coding agent" might inherit from "BaseAgent" and override methods. A "gate
    19→plugin" might register with a "gate manager." Each new capability requires understanding
    20→the framework's extension points.
    21→
    22→Roko takes a fundamentally different approach: **there are exactly six operations the system
    23→can perform on Engrams**, and every capability is one of those six operations. The six
    24→traits are:
    25→
    26→| Trait | Role | Async? | Primary Layer |
    27→|---|---|---|---|
    28→| **Substrate** | Persist and query Engrams | async | L0 Runtime |
    29→| **Scorer** | Rate Engrams along multiple axes | sync | L2 Scaffold |
    30→| **Gate** | Check Engrams against ground truth (returns Verdict) | async | L3 Harness |
    31→| **Router** | Choose best Engram from candidates (+ `feedback()`) | sync | L1 Framework |
    32→| **Composer** | Combine Engrams under budget constraints (takes `&dyn Scorer`) | sync | L2 Scaffold |
    33→| **Policy** | Observe Engram streams, emit new Engrams (batch input) | sync | L3-L4 |
    34→
    35→These traits are **distributed across layers** — they do not all live at one level. The
    36→architecture works because traits compose across layers rather than competing with them.
    37→
    38→### 1.1 Why Six
    39→
    40→The number six is not arbitrary. It emerged from analyzing the complete Roko design corpus
    41→(400+ capabilities across coding agents, chain agents, verification pipelines, context
    42→engineering, learning systems, and orchestration). Every capability, without exception,
    43→reduces to one of these six operations:
    44→
    45→1. **Store/retrieve** (Substrate) — every system needs persistence
    46→2. **Evaluate** (Scorer) — every system needs quality assessment
    47→3. **Verify** (Gate) — every system needs external truth checking
    48→4. **Choose** (Router) — every system needs selection among alternatives
    49→5. **Assemble** (Composer) — every system needs to combine information
    50→6. **React** (Policy) — every system needs reactive behavior
    51→
    52→This analysis is documented in detail in the original unified primitives design document
    53→(see `roko-progress/12-unified-primitives.md`), which shows how tool dispatch, model routing,
    54→context assembly, verification, conductor watchers, learning, chain participation, and
    55→observability all map to exactly these six trait interfaces.
    56→
    57→### 1.2 Why Traits (Not Objects, Not Functions)
    58→
    59→Rust traits provide three properties essential to Roko's design:
    60→
    61→1. **Static dispatch when possible**: Concrete trait implementations can be monomorphized
    62→   by the compiler, eliminating virtual dispatch overhead for hot paths.
    63→2. **Dynamic dispatch when needed**: `&dyn Trait` enables runtime composition — swapping
    64→   implementations based on configuration or learned preferences.
    65→3. **Bounds checking at compile time**: `Send + Sync` bounds on all traits ensure that
    66→   implementations are safe for concurrent use, which is required because the three
    67→   cognitive speeds (Gamma/Theta/Delta) run on separate async tasks.
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## 2. Substrate — Store and Query
    72→
    73→```rust
    74→/// Stores and queries Engrams.
    75→///
    76→/// All storage backends implement this trait: MemorySubstrate (testing),
    77→/// FileSubstrate (.roko/ persistence), HdcSubstrate (semantic search),
    78→/// ChainSubstrate (shared on-chain state). They are API-identical from a
    79→/// caller's perspective.
    80→///
    81→/// # Idempotence
    82→/// put is idempotent for Engrams with identical content hashes.
    83→///
    84→/// # Concurrency
    85→/// Substrates are Send + Sync. Impls must handle concurrent access.
    86→#[async_trait]
    87→pub trait Substrate: Send + Sync {
    88→    /// Store an Engram. Returns its content hash. Idempotent on content.
    89→    async fn put(&self, signal: Signal) -> Result<ContentHash>;
    90→
    91→    /// Retrieve an Engram by content hash. Does not apply decay.
    92→    async fn get(&self, id: &ContentHash) -> Result<Option<Signal>>;
    93→
    94→    /// Query for Engrams matching the given filter. Impls may apply decay
    95→    /// when evaluating min_weight and when ordering results.
    96→    async fn query(&self, q: &Query, ctx: &Context) -> Result<Vec<Signal>>;
    97→
    98→    /// Remove Engrams whose effective weight (score × decay) has fallen
    99→    /// below threshold at ctx.now_ms. Returns count of pruned Engrams.
   100→    async fn prune(&self, threshold: f32, ctx: &Context) -> Result<usize>;
   101→
   102→    /// Optional: total count of stored Engrams (for metrics/health checks).
   103→    async fn len(&self) -> Result<usize> { Ok(0) }
   104→
   105→    /// Optional: is the substrate empty?
   106→    async fn is_empty(&self) -> Result<bool> { Ok(self.len().await? == 0) }
   107→
   108→    /// Human-readable name for logging/debugging.
   109→    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "unnamed_substrate" }
   110→}
   111→```
   112→
   113→### 2.1 Why Async
   114→
   115→Substrate operations are async because storage backends may involve I/O:
   116→- **FileSubstrate** reads/writes JSONL files
   117→- **ChainSubstrate** makes RPC calls to read on-chain state
   118→- **HdcSubstrate** performs similarity search over HDC vectors
   119→- **NetworkSubstrate** (future) queries remote Substrates via the Agent Mesh
   120→
   121→Even `MemorySubstrate` (in-memory, used for testing) implements the async interface for
   122→compatibility.
   123→
   124→### 2.2 Implementations
   125→
   126→| Implementation | Backend | Layer | Use Case |
   127→|---|---|---|---|
   128→| `MemorySubstrate` | In-memory HashMap | L0 | Testing, ephemeral storage |
   129→| `FileSubstrate` | JSONL files in `.roko/` | L0 | Default persistence |
   130→| `HdcSubstrate` | HDC vectors + Hamming search | L0 | Semantic similarity queries |
   131→| `ChainSubstrate` | On-chain state via RPC | L1 | Shared agent state on Korai |
   132→
   133→### 2.3 Idempotence
   134→
   135→`put()` is idempotent: storing the same Engram twice (same ContentHash) is a no-op. This
   136→is a direct consequence of content-addressed storage — the identity IS the content.
   137→
   138→### 2.4 Pruning
   139→
   140→`prune()` removes Engrams whose `weight_at(ctx.now_ms) < threshold`. This is how the
   141→system implements automatic memory management. The threshold is configurable; typical values
   142→are 0.01 (aggressive pruning) to 0.001 (conservative).
   143→
   144→---
   145→
   146→## 3. Scorer — Rate
   147→
   148→```rust
   149→/// Rates an Engram along multi-dimensional axes.
   150→///
   151→/// Scorers are pure functions of (Engram, Context). They compose freely:
   152→/// use CompositeScorer to combine several scorers via +/× operations.
   153→///
   154→/// # Examples of Scorers
   155→/// - RelevanceScorer: how well does this Engram match the current goal?
   156→/// - RecencyScorer: how recent is this Engram?
   157→/// - ReputationScorer: how trustworthy is its author?
   158→/// - CatalyticScorer: how many downstream Engrams does this enable?
   159→pub trait Scorer: Send + Sync {
   160→    /// Score an Engram in the given context.
   161→    fn score(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Score;
   162→
   163→    /// Human-readable name.
   164→    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "unnamed_scorer" }
   165→}
   166→```
   167→
   168→### 3.1 Why Sync
   169→
   170→Scorers are synchronous because scoring should be fast — no I/O, no network calls, no
   171→blocking. A Scorer is a pure function: given an Engram and a Context, it produces a Score.
   172→This purity enables aggressive caching and parallelization.
   173→
   174→### 3.2 Composition
   175→
   176→Multiple Scorers compose into a single scoring pipeline via `CompositeScorer`:
   177→
   178→```rust
   179→// Example: composite scoring pipeline
   180→let scorer = CompositeScorer::new()
   181→    .add(RelevanceScorer::new(), 0.4)   // 40% weight
   182→    .add(RecencyScorer::new(), 0.3)     // 30% weight
   183→    .add(ReputationScorer::new(), 0.3); // 30% weight
   184→```
   185→
   186→Individual Scorer outputs combine via Score addition (evidence aggregation) or
   187→multiplication (modifier application), depending on the composition strategy.
   188→
   189→### 3.3 Scorer as Argument
   190→
   191→The Composer trait takes `&dyn Scorer` as a parameter, enabling the Composer to evaluate
   192→candidates using whatever scoring function is appropriate for the context. This is a key
   193→composition point: the same Composer can produce different outputs depending on which Scorer
   194→is injected.
   195→
   196→---
   197→
   198→## 4. Gate — Verify
   199→
   200→```rust
   201→/// Verifies an Engram against ground truth, producing a Verdict.
   202→///
   203→/// Gates are the bridge to external reality: compile, run tests, simulate
   204→/// transactions, check balances, validate schemas. A gate that returns
   205→/// passed = true is a claim that the Engram is correct in some domain.
   206→///
   207→/// # Async by default
   208→/// Gates typically invoke subprocesses, HTTP calls, or chain RPCs.
   209→#[async_trait]
   210→pub trait Gate: Send + Sync {
   211→    /// Verify the Engram and return a verdict.
   212→    async fn verify(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict;
   213→
   214→    /// Human-readable name (appears in verdicts).
   215→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   216→}
   217→```
   218→
   219→### 4.1 Why Async
   220→
   221→Gates invoke external processes:
   222→- **CompileGate** runs `cargo build`
   223→- **TestGate** runs `cargo test`
   224→- **ClippyGate** runs `cargo clippy`
   225→- **SimulationGate** simulates transactions via `eth_call`
   226→- **DiffGate** checks that file changes are within expected bounds
   227→
   228→All of these involve subprocess spawning and I/O, requiring async.
   229→
   230→### 4.2 Verdict (Not Boolean)
   231→
   232→Gates return a `Verdict`, not a boolean. The Verdict struct carries evidence:
   233→
   234→```rust
   235→pub struct Verdict {
   236→    pub passed: bool,
   237→    pub reason: String,
   238→    pub gate: String,
   239→    pub score: f32,           // [0..1] — for thresholding
   240→    pub detail: Option<String>,
   241→    pub test_count: Option<TestCount>,
   242→    pub error_digest: Option<String>,
   243→    pub duration_ms: u64,
   244→}
   245→```
   246→
   247→This rich output enables downstream Policies to make intelligent decisions: retry with
   248→different input, escalate to a human, adjust the approach based on specific failure modes.
   249→
   250→### 4.3 Gate Pipeline
   251→
   252→The `roko-gate` crate implements an 11-gate pipeline organized into 6 rungs:
   253→
   254→| Rung | Gate | What It Checks |
   255→|---|---|---|
   256→| 1 | CompileGate | Does the code compile? |
   257→| 2 | TestGate | Do tests pass? |
   258→| 3 | ClippyGate | Lint warnings? |
   259→| 4 | DiffGate | Are file changes within bounds? |
   260→| 5 | FormatGate | Is the code formatted? |
   261→| 6 | Custom gates | Domain-specific verification |
   262→
   263→Gates have adaptive thresholds (EMA per rung) that adjust based on observed pass rates.
   264→
   265→---
   266→
   267→## 5. Router — Select
   268→
   269→```rust
   270→/// Selects one Engram from many candidates.
   271→///
   272→/// Routers are the decision-making layer: which model to call, which backend
   273→/// to use, which gate to run next, which bounty to claim. They learn via
   274→/// feedback() so they improve with experience.
   275→///
   276→/// # Implementations
   277→/// - StaticRouter — deterministic choice (config-driven)
   278→/// - LinUCBRouter — contextual bandit
   279→/// - CascadeRouter — multi-stage confidence → UCB
   280→/// - WeightedRouter — softmax over scorers
   281→pub trait Router: Send + Sync {
   282→    /// Select one Engram from candidates. None = no selection made.
   283→    fn select(&self, candidates: &[Signal], ctx: &Context) -> Option<Selection>;
   284→
   285→    /// Learn from a selection's actual outcome.
   286→    fn feedback(&self, outcome: &Outcome);
   287→
   288→    /// Human-readable name (appears in selections).
   289→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   290→}
   291→```
   292→
   293→### 5.1 The feedback() Method
   294→
   295→The `feedback()` method is what makes Routers learn. After a selection is acted upon, the
   296→outcome (success/failure, reward, cost, latency) is fed back to the Router. This enables:
   297→
   298→- **CascadeRouter**: Updates contextual bandit weights based on reward
   299→- **LinUCBRouter**: Updates the LinUCB algorithm's confidence bounds
   300→- **EpsilonGreedyBandit**: Updates arm reward estimates
   301→
   302→The feedback loop is the core learning mechanism for model routing — the system improves its
   303→model selection over time without any manual tuning.
   304→
   305→### 5.2 Selection Output
   306→
   307→```rust
   308→pub struct Selection {
   309→    pub chosen: ContentHash,
   310→    pub confidence: f32,
   311→    pub router: String,
   312→    pub reasoning: Option<String>,
   313→}
   314→```
   315→
   316→The Selection identifies which Engram was chosen, how confident the Router is in that choice,
   317→and optionally why. This is logged for observability and used by the feedback loop.
   318→
   319→### 5.3 Outcome Feedback
   320→
   321→```rust
   322→pub struct Outcome {
   323→    pub selection: Selection,
   324→    pub success: bool,
   325→    pub reward: f32,
   326→    pub cost: Option<f32>,
   327→    pub latency_ms: Option<u64>,
   328→}
   329→```
   330→
   331→Outcomes feed back into the Router. Cost-aware Routers (like CascadeRouter) use the cost
   332→field to balance quality against expense, implementing the FrugalGPT cascade pattern
   333→(Chen et al. 2023, arXiv:2305.05176).
   334→
   335→---
   336→
   337→## 6. Composer — Combine
   338→
   339→```rust
   340→/// Combines multiple Engrams into one new Engram under a Budget.
   341→///
   342→/// Composers are the assembly layer: prompts from sections, context packs
   343→/// from fragments, transactions from operations, plans from tasks, bounties
   344→/// from sub-bounties. Output respects budget constraints.
   345→pub trait Composer: Send + Sync {
   346→    /// Combine input Engrams into a new composed Engram.
   347→    /// The composer may use the scorer to rank/select inputs under budget.
   348→    fn compose(
   349→        &self,
   350→        signals: &[Signal],
   351→        budget: &Budget,
   352→        scorer: &dyn Scorer,
   353→        ctx: &Context,
   354→    ) -> Result<Signal>;
   355→
   356→    /// Human-readable name.
   357→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   358→}
   359→```
   360→
   361→### 6.1 Budget Constraints
   362→
   363→```rust
   364→pub struct Budget {
   365→    pub max_tokens: Option<usize>,
   366→    pub max_signals: Option<usize>,
   367→    pub max_bytes: Option<usize>,
   368→    pub max_wall_ms: Option<u64>,
   369→}
   370→```
   371→
   372→Every Composer must respect the Budget. This is how Roko handles the fundamental constraint
   373→of LLM context windows — the Composer selects which Engrams to include and which to drop
   374→based on the Scorer's ranking, under the token/byte/count budget.
   375→
   376→### 6.2 Scorer Injection
   377→
   378→The Composer takes `&dyn Scorer` as a parameter, enabling different scoring strategies for
   379→different composition tasks. A prompt Composer might use a RelevanceScorer to prioritize
   380→context that matches the current goal, while a knowledge Composer might use a RecencyScorer
   381→to prioritize fresh information.
   382→
   383→### 6.3 Implementations
   384→
   385→| Composer | What It Assembles |
   386→|---|---|
   387→| `PromptComposer` | Assembles prompt sections into a complete prompt under token budget |
   388→| `ContextComposer` | Builds context packs from code files, documentation, and history |
   389→| `SystemPromptBuilder` | 6-layer prompt assembly with role templates |
   390→| `PlanComposer` | Combines task descriptions into execution plans |
   391→
   392→---
   393→
   394→## 7. Policy — React
   395→
   396→```rust
   397→/// Watches a stream of Engrams and emits new Engrams in response.
   398→///
   399→/// Policies are the reactive/behavioral layer: conductor watchers, circuit
   400→/// breakers, episode logging, pheromone reactions, heartbeat emission,
   401→/// promotion to chain, sentinel detection. They run continuously over the
   402→/// Engram stream and may produce zero, one, or many output Engrams per tick.
   403→pub trait Policy: Send + Sync {
   404→    /// Examine the recent Engram stream and produce new Engrams (interventions).
   405→    fn decide(&self, stream: &[Signal], ctx: &Context) -> Vec<Signal>;
   406→
   407→    /// Human-readable name.
   408→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   409→}
   410→```
   411→
   412→### 7.1 Batch Input
   413→
   414→Unlike Scorers (which operate on a single Engram) or Gates (which verify a single Engram),
   415→Policies receive a batch of recent Engrams — the "stream." This enables pattern detection
   416→across multiple Engrams:
   417→
   418→- **CircuitBreakerPolicy**: Monitors for repeated gate failures, emits a pause Engram
   419→- **EpisodeLogPolicy**: Collects agent turns and gate results, emits Episode Engrams
   420→- **PheromonePolicy**: Watches for market events, emits pheromone signals
   421→- **HeartbeatPolicy**: Emits periodic heartbeat Engrams for health monitoring
   422→- **AlertPolicy**: Watches for tool health degradation, emits ToolHealthDegraded Engrams
   423→
   424→### 7.2 Zero to Many Outputs
   425→
   426→A Policy may produce any number of output Engrams per invocation:
   427→- **Zero**: Nothing notable happened in the stream
   428→- **One**: A single intervention (e.g., circuit breaker trip)
   429→- **Many**: Multiple reactions (e.g., episode log + metric update + alert)
   430→
   431→---
   432→
   433→## 8. Trait × Layer Map
   434→
   435→Traits are distributed across the five architectural layers:
   436→
   437→```
   438→Layer 4: Orchestration  │ Policy (state machine transitions, plan reactions)
   439→Layer 3: Harness        │ Gate (verification), Policy (conductor watchers)
   440→Layer 2: Scaffold       │ Scorer (relevance), Composer (prompt assembly)
   441→Layer 1: Framework      │ Router (model selection), Scorer (tool relevance)
   442→Layer 0: Runtime        │ Substrate (persistence)
   443→```
   444→
   445→Key insight: the same trait can have implementations at different layers. A Scorer at L1
   446→(tool relevance for dispatch) is different from a Scorer at L2 (context relevance for prompt
   447→assembly), but both implement the same `Scorer` trait. This is how composition works across
   448→layers.
   449→
   450→---
   451→
   452→## 9. Composability Example
   453→
   454→A complete cognitive tick composes all six traits:
   455→
   456→```rust
   457→pub async fn loop_tick(
   458→    substrate: &dyn Substrate,
   459→    scorer: &dyn Scorer,
   460→    router: &dyn Router,
   461→    composer: &dyn Composer,
   462→    gate: &dyn Gate,
   463→    policy: &dyn Policy,
   464→    query: &Query,
   465→    budget: &Budget,
   466→    ctx: &Context,
   467→) -> Result<TickOutcome> {
   468→    // 1. Query the substrate for candidates.
   469→    let candidates = substrate.query(query, ctx).await?;
   470→
   471→    // 2. Router selects one candidate.
   472→    let Some(selection) = router.select(&candidates, ctx) else {
   473→        return Ok(TickOutcome { /* empty */ });
   474→    };
   475→
   476→    // 3. Composer builds a new Engram from the selection.
   477→    let chosen = candidates.iter().find(|s| s.id == selection.chosen).cloned();
   478→    let composed = composer.compose(&[chosen.unwrap()], budget, scorer, ctx)?;
   479→
   480→    // 4. Gate verifies the composition.
   481→    let verdict = gate.verify(&composed, ctx).await;
   482→
   483→    // 5. If passed, persist and run policy reaction.
   484→    if verdict.passed {
   485→        substrate.put(composed.clone()).await?;
   486→        let reactions = policy.decide(std::slice::from_ref(&composed), ctx);
   487→        for r in reactions {
   488→            substrate.put(r).await?;
   489→        }
   490→    }
   491→
   492→    Ok(TickOutcome { /* populated */ })
   493→}
   494→```
   495→
   496→Different agents use different implementations of each trait, but the loop structure is
   497→universal. This is the Synapse Architecture: one loop, six extension points, infinite
   498→combinations.
   499→
   500→---
   501→
   502→## 10. Categorical Analysis: Traits as Morphisms
   503→
   504→The six Synapse traits can be analyzed through the lens of category theory, revealing deep
   505→structural properties that explain why the architecture composes so cleanly.
   506→
   507→### 10.1 The Engram Category
   508→
   509→Define a category **Eng** where:
   510→- **Objects** are types that appear in the pipeline: `Vec<Signal>`, `Signal`, `Score`, `Selection`, `Verdict`, `Vec<Signal>` (reactions)
   511→- **Morphisms** are the trait operations:
   512→  - `query : () → Vec<Signal>` (Substrate)
   513→  - `score : Signal → Score` (Scorer)
   514→  - `select : Vec<Signal> → Selection` (Router)
   515→  - `compose : Vec<Signal> → Signal` (Composer)
   516→  - `verify : Signal → Verdict` (Gate)
   517→  - `decide : Vec<Signal> → Vec<Signal>` (Policy)
   518→
   519→The pipeline `query >> select >> compose >> verify >> persist >> decide` is a composition of
   520→morphisms in **Eng**. Category theory guarantees that this composition is:
   521→- **Associative**: `(f >> g) >> h = f >> (g >> h)` — the pipeline can be chunked arbitrarily
   522→- **Has identity**: the NoOp implementations form identity morphisms at each type
   523→
   524→### 10.2 Score as a Monoid
   525→
   526→`Score` forms a monoid under both addition and multiplication:
   527→
   528→```
   529→(Score, +, Score::ZERO)     — additive monoid (evidence aggregation)
   530→(Score, ×, Score::NEUTRAL)  — multiplicative monoid (modifier application)
   531→```
   532→
   533→Monoid structure guarantees that `fold` over any collection of Scores is well-defined and
   534→associative, enabling parallel reduction. CompositeScorer is precisely `fold` over a weighted
   535→collection of Scorer outputs.
   536→
   537→### 10.3 Cross-Cuts as Functors
   538→
   539→The three cognitive cross-cuts (Neuro, Daimon, Dreams) can be understood as **endofunctors**
   540→on the Engram category — structure-preserving transformations that enrich the pipeline:
   541→
   542→| Cross-Cut | Functorial Action | Preservation |
   543→|---|---|---|
   544→| **Neuro** | Enriches query results with knowledge | Preserves trait interfaces; any Substrate ↦ knowledge-enriched Substrate |
   545→| **Daimon** | Biases routing decisions with affect | Preserves Router interface; any Router ↦ affect-weighted Router |
   546→| **Dreams** | Transforms online pipeline to offline consolidation | Preserves loop structure; same 9 steps at Delta timescale |
   547→
   548→A **natural transformation** between functors would be a uniform way to convert between
   549→cross-cut perspectives — for instance, converting Daimon's PAD assessment into a Score
   550→modification that Neuro can store. The cross-cut interaction model (Section 5 of
   551→13-cognitive-cross-cuts.md) implicitly defines these natural transformations.
   552→
   553→### 10.4 Pipeline Composition as Monoid Homomorphism
   554→
   555→If the output of each trait implementation forms a monoid, then composing trait pipelines
   556→is itself monoidal. This gives **hierarchical composition for free**:
   557→
   558→```
   559→Gamma pipeline = query >> score >> route >> compose >> verify >> persist >> react
   560→Theta pipeline = fold(recent Gamma outcomes) >> route >> compose >> verify >> persist
   561→Delta pipeline = fold(recent Theta outcomes) >> route >> compose >> verify >> persist
   562→```
   563→
   564→Each higher-frequency pipeline's outputs are `fold`ed into inputs for the next level. The
   565→monoid structure guarantees this fold is well-defined regardless of how many Gamma ticks
   566→feed into a Theta tick.
   567→
   568→### 10.5 Implications for a 7th Trait
   569→
   570→Category theory suggests that the six traits are sufficient if and only if every morphism
   571→in **Eng** factors through them. The analysis in Section 23 (architectural-analysis-improvements)
   572→identifies two candidate operations — **Transform** (Signal → Signal mapping without budget)
   573→and **Observe** (read-only projection for telemetry) — that currently piggyback on existing
   574→traits. Both can be expressed as degenerate cases of Composer and Policy respectively, so the
   575→six traits remain sufficient. However, if the system grows a class of Signal-to-Signal
   576→transformations that are neither budget-constrained (Composer) nor stream-reactive (Policy),
   577→a dedicated `Transform` trait may become warranted.
   578→
   579→**References**:
   580→- Milewski, B. (2014). "Category Theory for Programmers." [bartoszmilewski.com](https://bartoszmilewski.com/2014/10/28/category-theory-for-programmers-the-preface/)
   581→- Seemann, M. (2017). "From Design Patterns to Category Theory." [blog.ploeh.dk](https://blog.ploeh.dk/2017/10/04/from-design-patterns-to-category-theory/)
   582→- Clarke et al. (2020). "Profunctor Optics: A Categorical Update." arXiv:2001.07488
   583→
   584→---
   585→
   586→## 11. Sufficiency Analysis: Is Six Enough?
   587→
   588→The six traits emerged from analyzing 400+ capabilities. But does the architecture handle
   589→edge cases cleanly? Analysis reveals three boundary areas:
   590→
   591→### 11.1 Operations That Fit But Are Awkward
   592→
   593→| Operation | Current Trait | Awkwardness |
   594→|---|---|---|
   595→| Signal transformation (e.g., summarize) | Composer with budget=∞ | Budget parameter is meaningless for 1:1 transforms |
   596→| Telemetry/observability emission | Policy with empty input | Policy signature expects a stream; observability just emits |
   597→| Batch verification (multiple signals) | Gate called in loop | Gate verifies one signal; batch verification requires external loop |
   598→
   599→### 11.2 Why Not a 7th Trait?
   600→
   601→A `Transform` trait (`Signal → Signal`, no budget, no stream) would capture 1:1 signal
   602→transformations cleanly. However:
   603→
   604→1. **Parsimony**: Every additional trait multiplies the composition surface. Six traits
   605→   produce 6! = 720 ordering permutations; seven produce 5,040.
   606→2. **Degenerate Composer**: `Composer::compose(&[single_signal], &Budget::UNLIMITED, ...)` is
   607→   functionally identical to a Transform. The budget parameter is unused but causes no harm.
   608→3. **Cognitive simplicity**: "One noun, six verbs" is a powerful mnemonic. Adding a seventh
   609→   verb dilutes the conceptual model.
   610→
   611→**Decision**: Keep six. The awkward cases are minor and the parsimony benefit is substantial.
   612→If a future domain produces a large class of budget-free, stream-free Signal transformations,
   613→revisit this decision.
   614→
   615→### 11.3 Could Traits Be Merged?
   616→
   617→| Candidate Merge | Argument For | Argument Against |
   618→|---|---|---|
   619→| Scorer + Router | Both evaluate signals | Router has feedback(); Scorer is stateless |
   620→| Gate + Scorer | Both produce quality assessments | Gate is async, external, produces Verdict; Scorer is sync, internal, produces Score |
   621→| Policy + Gate | Both watch outputs | Policy is reactive (many→many); Gate is verificatory (one→Verdict) |
   622→
   623→No merge candidates are compelling. The sync/async, stateful/stateless, and one/many
   624→distinctions justify keeping each trait separate.
   625→

1→# Collective Emotional Contagion
     2→
     3→> How emotional state propagates across the agent mesh with exponential decay, somatic field formation, and anti-cascade safeguards.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Built
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Daimon](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: [01-pad-vector.md](./01-pad-vector.md), [06-somatic-markers-damasio.md](./06-somatic-markers-damasio.md)
    10→**Key sources**: `bardo-backup/prd/03-daimon/07-runtime-daimon.md` §5, `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-subsystems.md` §2, `refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md` §III
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→When agents share a mesh (collective), their emotional states interact. An agent that discovers a critical issue (high arousal) influences mesh peers to raise their own vigilance. An agent experiencing sustained success (high pleasure) influences peers toward confidence. This emotional contagion is not metaphorical — it is a concrete data flow where PAD deltas propagate between agents with specific attenuation factors, caps, and decay rates.
    17→
    18→The contagion mechanism is carefully constrained to prevent cascading panic or cascading overconfidence. Pleasure and arousal attenuate to 30% of the source intensity. Dominance does not propagate at all — an agent's sense of control is always locally determined. Arousal is capped at +0.3 per sync cycle. Propagation is unidirectional (no reciprocal feedback). Borrowed emotions decay with a 6-hour half-life unless reinforced by the agent's own experience.
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Contagion Rules
    23→
    24→### Attenuation Factors
    25→
    26→| Dimension | Attenuation | Rationale |
    27→|---|---|---|
    28→| **Pleasure** | 0.3 (30%) | A mesh peer's success is informative but not as significant as your own success. 30% preserves the signal while preventing emotional overreaction to others' outcomes. |
    29→| **Arousal** | 0.3 (30%) | A peer's urgency should increase your vigilance but not overwhelm your own assessment. 30% creates awareness without panic. |
    30→| **Dominance** | 0.0 (no propagation) | Control perception is strictly local. Knowing that a peer feels confident doesn't make *you* more capable. Dominance must be earned through your own outcomes. |
    31→
    32→### Cap per Sync Cycle
    33→
    34→| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
    35→|---|---|---|
    36→| Arousal cap per sync | +0.3 | Even if multiple peers send alarming data simultaneously, the receiving agent's arousal increase is bounded. Prevents cascading panic where N agents all alarm each other to maximum arousal. |
    37→
    38→### Decay
    39→
    40→| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
    41→|---|---|---|
    42→| Contagion decay half-life | 6 hours | Borrowed emotions dissipate quickly unless reinforced by the agent's own experience. After 12 hours without reinforcement, contagion effects are at 25% of original strength (two half-lives). |
    43→
    44→### Propagation Direction
    45→
    46→| Property | Value | Rationale |
    47→|---|---|---|
    48→| Direction | Unidirectional | Agent A's contagion to Agent B does NOT cause Agent B to emit contagion back to Agent A. This prevents positive feedback loops where two agents continuously amplify each other's emotional state. |
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Contagion Triggers
    53→
    54→Emotional contagion is not continuous — it fires on specific events that one agent shares with the mesh:
    55→
    56→| Trigger | Emotional Effect on Receiver | Source |
    57→|---|---|---|
    58→| **Peer warning push** | Arousal +0.1 (capped) | Agent shares a Warning-type Engram with the mesh |
    59→| **Peer alert** (critical issue) | Arousal +0.1 (capped) | Agent detects a significant anomaly |
    60→| **Peer sustained success** | Dominance +0.05 | Agent reports consistently high task success rate |
    61→| **Peer sustained failure** | Pleasure -0.05 | Agent reports consistently low task success rate |
    62→| **Peer dream insight** | Arousal +0.05 | Agent's dream cycle produces a validated insight |
    63→
    64→### Warning Push Example
    65→
    66→When Agent A discovers a critical issue and pushes a Warning to the mesh:
    67→
    68→```rust
    69→// Agent A: discovers critical issue
    70→let warning = Engram::new(
    71→    Kind::Warning,
    72→    "roko-gate compile check failing on stable channel due to MSRV bump",
    73→);
    74→mesh.push_warning(warning);  // shared with mesh peers
    75→
    76→// Agent B: receives the warning
    77→// Contagion trigger fires:
    78→agent_b.daimon.apply_contagion(ContagionEvent {
    79→    source: agent_a.id,
    80→    trigger: ContagionTrigger::WarningPush,
    81→    source_pad: agent_a.daimon.query().pad,
    82→});
    83→```
    84→
    85→### Contagion Application
    86→
    87→```rust
    88→impl DaimonState {
    89→    pub fn apply_contagion(&mut self, event: ContagionEvent) {
    90→        let source = event.source_pad;
    91→
    92→        // Attenuate: P and A at 30%, D at 0%
    93→        let p_delta = source.pleasure * 0.3;
    94→        let a_delta = source.arousal * 0.3;
    95→        // D never propagates
    96→
    97→        // Cap arousal at +0.3 per sync cycle
    98→        let a_capped = a_delta.min(0.3);
    99→
   100→        let now = Utc::now();
   101→        self.state.apply_delta(p_delta, a_capped, 0.0, 0.0, now);
   102→
   103→        // Mark this delta as borrowed (subject to accelerated decay)
   104→        self.borrowed_affect.push(BorrowedAffect {
   105→            source: event.source,
   106→            p_delta,
   107→            a_delta: a_capped,
   108→            applied_at: now,
   109→        });
   110→    }
   111→}
   112→```
   113→
   114→---
   115→
   116→## Anti-Cascade Design
   117→
   118→### The Cascade Problem
   119→
   120→Without safeguards, emotional contagion produces cascading amplification:
   121→
   122→```
   123→Agent A detects anomaly → arousal spike
   124→  → Shares with mesh
   125→    → Agent B receives contagion → arousal increases
   126→      → Agent B shares its heightened state
   127→        → Agent A receives contagion from B → arousal increases further
   128→          → (positive feedback loop → all agents at maximum arousal)
   129→```
   130→
   131→This is analogous to financial panic propagation, where fear of a bank run causes a bank run. The contagion mechanism must prevent this while preserving the useful signal (legitimate anomalies should raise collective vigilance).
   132→
   133→### Three Anti-Cascade Mechanisms
   134→
   135→**Mechanism 1: Cap per sync cycle (+0.3 arousal max)**
   136→
   137→Even if every peer in the mesh sends an alarm simultaneously, the receiving agent's arousal increases by at most 0.3 per sync cycle. With a typical sync interval of 30 seconds, the maximum arousal ramp rate is 0.3/30s = 0.6/min. Combined with the 4-hour decay half-life, this limits the steady-state arousal contribution from contagion to approximately 0.4 (the level where decay rate equals accumulation rate).
   138→
   139→**Mechanism 2: Unidirectional propagation**
   140→
   141→Contagion flows one way: source → receiver. The receiver does not re-emit the borrowed emotional state. This breaks the feedback loop at the first hop — Agent A's alarm reaches Agent B, but Agent B's response to that alarm does not reach Agent A.
   142→
   143→If Agent B independently discovers the same anomaly, it will generate its own alarm through normal appraisal. This is desirable — it means the collective arousal reflects genuinely redundant detection, not echo amplification.
   144→
   145→**Mechanism 3: Rapid decay (6h half-life)**
   146→
   147→Borrowed emotions decay at a 6-hour half-life, which is faster than the standard 4-hour half-life applied to the agent's own emotional state. Wait — the borrowed decay is actually *slower* than self-generated state decay (6h vs. 4h). This is because borrowed emotions represent information from peers and should persist long enough to be acted on, but not so long that they dominate the agent's own experience.
   148→
   149→After 6 hours without reinforcement:
   150→```
   151→t=0h:   borrowed arousal = 0.30
   152→t=6h:   borrowed arousal = 0.15  (one half-life)
   153→t=12h:  borrowed arousal = 0.075 (two half-lives)
   154→t=18h:  borrowed arousal = 0.037 (three half-lives — negligible)
   155→```
   156→
   157→If the agent's own experience confirms the peer's alarm (the agent also detects the anomaly), the arousal is reinforced through the normal appraisal pipeline and no longer depends on the borrowed component.
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## Somatic Field Formation
   162→
   163→### From Individual Markers to Collective Landscape
   164→
   165→When multiple agents share a mesh, their individual somatic landscapes can be aggregated into a **somatic field** — a collective emotional memory over the shared strategy space. Each agent contributes its somatic markers to the field, creating a higher-resolution map of which strategies work and which don't.
   166→
   167→```rust
   168→pub struct SomaticField {
   169→    /// Merged k-d tree from all mesh members.
   170→    landscape: SomaticLandscape,
   171→    /// Contribution weights per agent.
   172→    agent_weights: HashMap<AgentId, f64>,
   173→}
   174→
   175→impl SomaticField {
   176→    /// Merge an agent's somatic landscape into the collective field.
   177→    pub fn merge(&mut self, agent_id: AgentId, markers: &[SomaticMarker]) {
   178→        let weight = self.agent_weights.get(&agent_id).copied().unwrap_or(1.0);
   179→
   180→        for marker in markers {
   181→            let weighted_marker = SomaticMarker {
   182→                strategy_coords: marker.strategy_coords,
   183→                valence: marker.valence * weight,
   184→                intensity: marker.intensity * weight,
   185→                episodes: marker.episodes.clone(),
   186→            };
   187→            self.landscape.tree.add(&marker.strategy_coords, weighted_marker);
   188→        }
   189→    }
   190→}
   191→```
   192→
   193→### Weight Calibration
   194→
   195→Agent contribution weights in the somatic field are calibrated by historical accuracy. An agent whose somatic markers correlate with actual outcomes gets higher weight; an agent whose markers are unreliable gets lower weight:
   196→
   197→```
   198→weight = (correct_predictions / total_predictions) × seniority_factor
   199→```
   200→
   201→This prevents a single unreliable agent from corrupting the collective emotional memory while allowing experienced, accurate agents to contribute proportionally more.
   202→
   203→### Privacy Boundary
   204→
   205→Somatic markers shared with the mesh contain only:
   206→- Strategy coordinates (8D vector)
   207→- Valence (positive/negative)
   208→- Intensity (strength)
   209→
   210→They do **not** contain:
   211→- Episode content (what actually happened)
   212→- Task details (what the agent was doing)
   213→- PAD vector (the agent's full emotional state)
   214→
   215→This preserves operational privacy while sharing the emotional signal. An agent can query the somatic field and learn "strategies in this region tend to go badly" without learning the specific failures that generated the markers.
   216→
   217→---
   218→
   219→## C-Factor Integration
   220→
   221→The C-Factor (Collective Intelligence Factor, from the collective intelligence literature) measures how well the mesh performs as a group beyond the sum of individual capabilities. Emotional contagion contributes to the C-Factor through two channels:
   222→
   223→1. **Collective vigilance**: When one agent raises an alarm, the entire mesh becomes more vigilant. This means the collective detects issues faster than any individual agent, even if only one agent directly encounters the anomaly.
   224→
   225→2. **Strategy diversification**: The somatic field enables agents to avoid strategies that peers have found unsuccessful. This reduces redundant failure — agents explore different regions of strategy space rather than all attempting the same approach.
   226→
   227→The C-Factor is not yet implemented, but emotional contagion is one of the mechanisms that would contribute to its measurement.
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Observability
   232→
   233→### Metrics
   234→
   235→The Daimon exports contagion-related metrics:
   236→
   237→| Metric | Type | Description |
   238→|---|---|---|
   239→| `roko_daimon_contagion_received_total` | Counter | Number of contagion events received |
   240→| `roko_daimon_contagion_arousal_cap_hits` | Counter | Times the arousal cap was reached |
   241→| `roko_daimon_borrowed_affect_active` | Gauge | Current count of active borrowed affect entries |
   242→
   243→### Alerts
   244→
   245→| Alert | Condition | Severity | Response |
   246→|---|---|---|---|
   247→| Contagion saturation | Arousal cap hit 3+ times in 24h | Info | Review mesh peer alert frequency |
   248→| Collective anxiety | >50% of mesh members in Struggling state | Warning | Check for systemic issue |
   249→| Contagion isolation | No contagion received in 48h | Info | Check mesh connectivity |
   250→
   251→---
   252→
   253→## Current Status and Gaps
   254→
   255→**Specified**: Full contagion rules in `07-runtime-daimon.md` §5. Attenuation factors, caps, decay, and anti-cascade mechanisms. Somatic field concept in `09-innovations.md` §III.
   256→
   257→**Not implemented**: No contagion code exists in `roko-daimon` or `roko-golem`. The agent mesh (Korai) is not yet built. Somatic field aggregation is not implemented. C-Factor measurement is not implemented.
   258→
   259→**Dependency**: Requires the agent mesh infrastructure (topic [04-knowledge](../06-neuro/INDEX.md)) for inter-agent communication. Requires the somatic landscape (see [06-somatic-markers-damasio.md](./06-somatic-markers-damasio.md)) for somatic field formation.
   260→
   261→---
   262→
   263→## Academic Foundations
   264→
   265→- Hatfield, E., Cacioppo, J.T., & Rapson, R.L. (1993). "Emotional contagion." *Current Directions in Psychological Science*, 2(3), 96–100.
   266→- Bower, G.H. (1981). "Mood and Memory." *American Psychologist*, 36(2), 129–148.
   267→- Woolley, A.W. et al. (2010). "Evidence for a Collective Intelligence Factor in the Performance of Human Groups." *Science*, 330(6004), 686–688.
   268→- Grassé, P.P. (1959). "La reconstruction du nid et les coordinations interindividuelles chez Bellicositermes natalensis et Cubitermes sp." *Insectes Sociaux*, 6(1), 41–80.
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→## Cross-references
   273→
   274→- See [01-pad-vector.md](./01-pad-vector.md) for PAD vector structure
   275→- See [06-somatic-markers-damasio.md](./06-somatic-markers-damasio.md) for individual somatic landscape
   276→- See [10-integration-points.md](./10-integration-points.md) for Daimon integration points
   277→- See topic [04-knowledge](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) for mesh infrastructure and Engram sharing
   278→

1→# Current Status and Gaps
     2→
     3→> What's built, what's scaffolded, what's specified but unimplemented, and which legacy files were deliberately skipped.
     4→
     5→
     6→> **Implementation**: Built
     7→
     8→**Topic**: [Daimon](./INDEX.md)
     9→**Prerequisites**: All previous sub-docs
    10→**Key sources**: `roko-daimon/src/lib.rs`, `roko-golem/src/daimon.rs`, `refactoring-prd/07-implementation-priorities.md`
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Abstract
    15→
    16→The Daimon affect engine exists in two parallel implementations (`roko-daimon` and `roko-golem/daimon.rs`) with significant overlap and some divergence. The core PAD vector, appraisal pipeline, and behavioral modulation are functional. The somatic landscape, collective contagion, VCG bidding integration, and several appraisal triggers are specified but not built. This document catalogs the exact state of each component, references the implementation priority tiers, and lists the legacy source files that were deliberately skipped during migration.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Implemented Components
    21→
    22→### roko-daimon (standalone crate)
    23→
    24→**File**: `crates/roko-daimon/src/lib.rs` (569 lines)
    25→
    26→| Component | Status | Description |
    27→|---|---|---|
    28→| `PadVector` struct | **Complete** | Three f64 fields (pleasure, arousal, dominance), clamped to [-1, 1] |
    29→| `AffectState` struct | **Complete** | PAD + confidence + updated_at timestamp |
    30→| `AffectEvent` enum | **Complete** | 6 variants: GateResult, TaskOutcome, Blocked, TimePressure, QueueWait, DreamFailure |
    31→| `DaimonState` struct | **Complete** | Wraps AffectState + half_life_hours + persistence_path |
    32→| `AffectEngine` trait | **Complete** | 4 methods: appraise(), query(), modulate(), persist() |
    33→| Appraisal rules | **Complete** | All 6 event types with correct PAD deltas, rung scaling, asymmetric valence |
    34→| Temporal decay | **Complete** | Exponential decay: `factor = 0.5 ^ (elapsed_hours / half_life_hours)` |
    35→| Behavioral modulation | **Complete** | Model promotion/demotion (haiku↔sonnet↔opus), turn limit adjustment, strategy selection |
    36→| Persistence | **Complete** | Atomic file write (write to .tmp, rename) with auto-save on appraise |
    37→| Load/restore | **Complete** | `load_or_new()` loads from disk or creates fresh neutral state |
    38→| `DispatchStrategy` enum | **Complete** | 5 variants with effort labels: Conservative, Balanced, Exploratory, Escalating, Proactive |
    39→| `DispatchParams` struct | **Complete** | model + turn_limit + strategy + effort |
    40→| `queue_wait_arousal()` | **Complete** | Public function for queue-wait arousal computation |
    41→| Tests | **Complete** | 3 tests: appraise/persist, escalation, demotion |
    42→
    43→### roko-golem/daimon.rs (per-task affect engine)
    44→
    45→**File**: `crates/roko-golem/src/daimon.rs` (972 lines)
    46→
    47→| Component | Status | Description |
    48→|---|---|---|

    50→| `AffectEngine` struct | **Complete** | Per-task HashMap<String, AffectState> + half_life + persistence |

    52→| `AffectBehaviorModulation` struct | **Complete** | 7 behavioral parameters (strategy, exploration_rate, prefer_proven_playbooks, model_tier_escalation, extra_retries, trigger_dream_cycles, run_maintenance_tasks) |
    53→| 5 behavior factory methods | **Complete** | balanced(), anxious(), confident(), angry(), bored() |
    54→| Per-task appraisal methods | **Complete** | on_task_success(), on_task_failure(), on_dream_failure(), on_gate_pass(), on_gate_fail(), on_time_pressure(), on_blocked(), queue_wait_arousal() |
    55→| Signal emission | **Complete** | Emits affect signals to JSONL when confidence drops below 0.3 or valence crosses extremes |
    56→| Atomic persistence | **Complete** | save_to() with tmp+rename pattern |
    57→| Tests | **Complete** | 13 tests covering all appraisal methods, octant classification, behavior modulation, decay, persistence |
    58→
    59→### Overlap Between the Two Implementations
    60→
    61→| Feature | roko-daimon | roko-golem/daimon.rs | Notes |
    62→|---|---|---|---|

    64→| Appraisal rules | Via `AffectEvent` enum match | Via named methods (on_gate_pass, etc.) | Same deltas, different API surface |
    65→| Behavioral modulation | `modulate()` on DispatchParams | `behavior_modulation()` on AffectOctant | Different output types |
    66→| Octant classification | Not present | `AffectOctant::from_pad()` | roko-golem only |
    67→| Per-task tracking | Not present (single global state) | `HashMap<String, AffectState>` | roko-golem only |

    71→
    72→### Consolidation Plan (Tier 0C)

    74→The plan (from `refactoring-prd/07-implementation-priorities.md` Tier 0C) is to dissolve `roko-golem` and move affect logic into `roko-daimon`. The consolidated crate should:

    83→---

    85→## Scaffolded Components

    87→These exist as types or stubs but are not wired into the runtime:

    89→| Component | Where | What Exists | What's Missing |

    93→| CascadeRouter threshold adjustment | roko-learn | Router exists, thresholds exist | Router doesn't read Daimon thresholds |
    94→
    95→---
    96→
    97→## Specified but Not Implemented
    98→
    99→These are fully specified in the legacy PRDs and/or `refactoring-prd` but have no code:
   100→
   101→### Implementation Plan Reference (12a-cognitive-layer.md)
   102→
   103→| Task | Description | Priority |
   104→|---|---|---|
   105→| F1 | `PadVector` struct and `AffectState` | **Done** |
   106→| F2 | 8 octant states from PAD sign | **Done** (roko-golem) |
   107→| F3 | `AffectEvent` enum and `AffectEngine::appraise()` | **Done** |
   108→| F4 | Temporal decay (exponential, 4h half-life) | **Done** |
   109→| F5 | Behavior modulation table | **Done** (both crates) |
   110→| F6 | Affect signatures on episodes | **Not done** — episodes don't carry EmotionalTag |
   111→| F7 | Affect → SystemPromptBuilder | **Not done** — Daimon doesn't write to prompt context |
   112→| F8 | Affect → CascadeRouter | **Not done** — router doesn't read Daimon thresholds |
   113→| F9 | Persistence (autosave + load) | **Done** |
   114→
   115→### Unimplemented Features by Category
   116→
   117→**Somatic Landscape**:
   118→- `SomaticLandscape` struct with k-d tree over 8D strategy space
   119→- `SomaticMarker` struct with valence, intensity, episode provenance

   124→- 15% contrarian retrieval within somatic queries

   127→- `kiddo` crate dependency
   128→
   129→**Emotional Memory Integration**:
   130→- `EmotionalTag` on Engrams
   131→- Four-factor retrieval scoring (recency × importance × relevance × emotional congruence)
   132→- PAD cosine similarity for retrieval scoring
   133→- Emotional provenance tracking on consolidated knowledge
   134→- Emotional diversity as quality signal
   135→- Consolidation priority boost for high-arousal episodes
   136→- Mind wandering mechanism (spontaneous retrieval every ~200 ticks)
   137→
   138→**Collective Contagion**:
   139→- Inter-agent emotional contagion protocol
   140→- P/A attenuation (0.3), D blocking (0.0)
   141→- Arousal cap per sync cycle (+0.3)
   142→- Unidirectional propagation enforcement
   143→- 6-hour borrowed-emotion decay
   144→- Somatic field aggregation across mesh
   145→- C-Factor measurement
   146→
   147→**VCG Auction Integration**:
   148→- Urgency multiplier from arousal: `urgency = 1 + arousal × 0.5`
   149→- Affect weight from pleasure extremes: `affect_weight = 1 + 0.3 × abs(pleasure - 0.5)`
   150→- Per-subsystem bid modulation based on PAD state
   151→
   152→**Coding Agent Integration**:
   153→- Per-crate confidence aggregation
   154→- Error pattern tracker with familiarity scoring
   155→- Fatigue detection (consecutive failure monitoring)
   156→- Error category extraction from gate output
   157→
   158→**Future Appraisal Triggers**:
   159→- Prediction accuracy (depends on Tier 2J)
   160→- Peer comparison (depends on Tier 2M)
   161→- Novel domain entry
